title: University of Cambridge

infobox.value: The Chancellor, Masters, and Scholars of the University of Cambridge[[4]](#cite_note-fullname-4)
Literal: From here, light and sacred draughts. Non-literal: From this place, we gain enlightenment and precious knowledge.

[Public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [collegiate](/wiki/Collegiate_university "Collegiate university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") [Ancient university](/wiki/Ancient_university "Ancient university")
[The Lord Smith of Finsbury](/wiki/Chris_Smith,_Baron_Smith_of_Finsbury "Chris Smith, Baron Smith of Finsbury")

* [ACU](/wiki/Association_of_Commonwealth_Universities "Association of Commonwealth Universities") * [EUA](/wiki/European_University_Association "European University Association") * [IARU](/wiki/International_Alliance_of_Research_Universities "International Alliance of Research Universities") * [LERU](/wiki/League_of_European_Research_Universities "League of European Research Universities") * [Russell Group](/wiki/Russell_Group "Russell Group") * [SES](/wiki/SES_(universities) "SES (universities)") * [Universities UK](/wiki/Universities_UK "Universities UK")

is a [collegiate](/wiki/Collegiate_university "Collegiate university") [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge"), England. Founded in 1209, the University of Cambridge is the [world's third-oldest university in continuous operation](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation"). The university's founding followed the arrival of scholars who left the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") for Cambridge after a dispute with local townspeople.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) The two [ancient](/wiki/Ancient_university "Ancient university") English universities, although sometimes described as rivals, share many common features and are often jointly referred to as [Oxbridge](/wiki/Oxbridge "Oxbridge").

The **University of Cambridge** is a [collegiate](/wiki/Collegiate_university "Collegiate university") [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge"), England. Founded in 1209, the University of Cambridge is the [world's third-oldest university in continuous operation](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation"). The university's founding followed the arrival of scholars who left the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") for Cambridge after a dispute with local townspeople.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) The two [ancient](/wiki/Ancient_university "Ancient university") English universities, although sometimes described as rivals, share many common features and are often jointly referred to as [Oxbridge](/wiki/Oxbridge "Oxbridge").

In 1231, 22 years after its founding, the university was recognised with a [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter"), granted by [King Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England"). The University of Cambridge includes [31 semi-autonomous constituent colleges](/wiki/Colleges_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Colleges of the University of Cambridge") and [more than 150 academic departments, faculties, and other institutions organised into six schools](/wiki/Schools_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Schools of the University of Cambridge"). The largest department is [Cambridge University Press and Assessment](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Press_and_Assessment "Cambridge University Press and Assessment"), which contains the oldest [university press](/wiki/University_press "University press") in the world, with £1 billion of annual revenue and with 100 million learners.[[13]](#cite_note-14) All of the colleges are self-governing institutions within the university, managing their own personnel and policies, and all students are required to have a college affiliation within the university. Undergraduate teaching at Cambridge is centred on weekly small-group [supervisions](/wiki/Tutorial_system "Tutorial system") in the colleges with lectures, seminars, laboratory work, and occasionally further supervision provided by the central university faculties and departments.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16)

The university operates eight cultural and scientific museums, including the [Fitzwilliam Museum](/wiki/Fitzwilliam_Museum "Fitzwilliam Museum") and [Cambridge University Botanic Garden](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Botanic_Garden "Cambridge University Botanic Garden"). [Cambridge's 116 libraries](/wiki/Libraries_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Libraries of the University of Cambridge") hold a total of approximately 16 million books, around 9 million of which are in [Cambridge University Library](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Library "Cambridge University Library"), a [legal deposit library](/wiki/Legal_deposit "Legal deposit") and one of the world's largest [academic libraries](/wiki/Academic_library "Academic library").

Among the university's [notable alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Cambridge_people "List of University of Cambridge people") are [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon "Francis Bacon"), [Lord Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron"), [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin"), [Rajiv Gandhi](/wiki/Rajiv_Gandhi "Rajiv Gandhi"), [John Harvard](/wiki/John_Harvard "John Harvard"), [Stephen Hawking](/wiki/Stephen_Hawking "Stephen Hawking"), [John Maynard Keynes](/wiki/John_Maynard_Keynes "John Maynard Keynes"), [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton"), [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov"), [Jawaharlal Nehru](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru "Jawaharlal Nehru"), [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton"), [Salman Rushdie](/wiki/Salman_Rushdie "Salman Rushdie"), [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell"), [Alan Turing](/wiki/Alan_Turing "Alan Turing"), [Ludwig Wittgenstein](/wiki/Ludwig_Wittgenstein "Ludwig Wittgenstein"), [William Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth"), and 194 [Olympic medal](/wiki/Olympic_medal "Olympic medal")-winning athletes.[[16]](#cite_note-Hawks-17) Its alumni, academics, and affiliates have won 126 [Nobel Prizes](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize").[[17]](#cite_note-18)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding")]

See also: [Timeline of Cambridge](/wiki/Timeline_of_Cambridge "Timeline of Cambridge")

Prior to the founding of the University of Cambridge in 1209, [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge") and the area surrounding it already had developed a scholarly and ecclesiastical reputation largely due to the intellectual and academic contributions of monks from the nearby [Ely Cathedral](/wiki/Ely_Cathedral "Ely Cathedral"). The founding of the university, however, mostly originated with an incident at the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") during which three Oxford scholars, following the death of a local woman, were [hanged](/wiki/Hanging "Hanging") by the town's secular authorities. The local ecclesiastical authorities, who were not consulted, would have traditionally been inclined to pardon scholars in such cases. During this time, Oxford's town authorities were in conflict with [King John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England"). Fearing more violence from Oxford townsfolk, University of Oxford scholars began leaving Oxford for more hospitable cities, including Paris, [Reading](/wiki/Reading,_Berkshire "Reading, Berkshire"), and [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge"). Enough scholars ultimately took residence in Cambridge to form, along with the many scholars already there, the nucleus for the new university's formation.[[18]](#cite_note-Early_records-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21)

By 1225, a chancellor of the university was appointed, and writs issued by [King Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England") in 1231 established that rents in Cambridge were to be set *secundum consuetudinem universitatis*, according to the custom of the university, and established a panel of two masters and two townsmen to determine these. A letter from [Pope Gregory IX](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_IX "Pope Gregory IX") two years later to the chancellor and the guild of scholars granted the new university *ius non trahi extra*, or the right not to be drawn out, for three years, meaning its members could not be summoned to a court outside of the diocese of Ely.[[21]](#cite_note-22) After Cambridge was described as a *[studium generale](/wiki/Studium_generale "Studium generale")* in a letter from [Pope Nicholas IV](/wiki/Pope_Nicholas_IV "Pope Nicholas IV") in 1290,[[22]](#cite_note-Hackett-23) and confirmed as such by [Pope John XXII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXII "Pope John XXII")'s 1318 [papal bull](/wiki/Papal_bull "Papal bull"),[[23]](#cite_note-24) it became common for researchers from other European [medieval universities](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university") to visit Cambridge to study or give lectures.[[22]](#cite_note-Hackett-23)

### Foundation of the colleges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Foundation of the colleges")]

See also: [Colleges of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Colleges_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Colleges of the University of Cambridge") and [Oxford and Cambridge Act 1571](/wiki/Oxford_and_Cambridge_Act_1571 "Oxford and Cambridge Act 1571")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Peterhouse_Chapel.jpg/250px-Peterhouse_Chapel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peterhouse_Chapel.jpg)

[Peterhouse](/wiki/Peterhouse,_Cambridge "Peterhouse, Cambridge"), Cambridge's first college, founded in 1284

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Emmanuel_College_Front_Court%2C_Cambridge%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg/250px-Emmanuel_College_Front_Court%2C_Cambridge%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emmanuel_College_Front_Court,_Cambridge,_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)

[Emmanuel College](/wiki/Emmanuel_College,_Cambridge "Emmanuel College, Cambridge") Chapel

The [31 colleges](/wiki/Colleges_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Colleges of the University of Cambridge") of the present-day University of Cambridge were originally an incidental feature of the university; no college within the University of Cambridge is as old as the university itself. The colleges within the university were initially endowed fellowships of scholars. There were also institutions without endowments, called hostels, which were gradually absorbed by the colleges over the centuries, and they have left some traces, including the naming of Garret Hostel Lane and [Garret Hostel Bridge](/wiki/Garret_Hostel_Bridge "Garret Hostel Bridge"), a street and bridge in Cambridge.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The University of Cambridge's first college, [Peterhouse](/wiki/Peterhouse,_Cambridge "Peterhouse, Cambridge"), was founded in 1284 by [Hugh de Balsham](/wiki/Hugh_de_Balsham "Hugh de Balsham"), the [Bishop of Ely](/wiki/Bishop_of_Ely "Bishop of Ely"). Multiple additional colleges were founded during the 14th and 15th centuries, and colleges continued to be established during modern times, though there was a 204-year gap between the founding of [Sidney Sussex](/wiki/Sidney_Sussex_College,_Cambridge "Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge") in 1596 and that of [Downing](/wiki/Downing_College,_Cambridge "Downing College, Cambridge") in 1800. The most recent college to be established is [Robinson](/wiki/Robinson_College,_Cambridge "Robinson College, Cambridge"), which was built in the late 1970s. Most recently, in March 2010, [Homerton College](/wiki/Homerton_College,_Cambridge "Homerton College, Cambridge") achieved full university college status, making it the university's newest full college.

In [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") times, many colleges were founded so that their members could [pray](/wiki/Prayer "Prayer") for the [souls](/wiki/Soul "Soul") of the founders. University of Cambridge colleges were often associated with chapels or [abbeys](/wiki/Abbey "Abbey"). The colleges' focus began to shift in 1536, however, with the [dissolution of the monasteries](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_monasteries "Dissolution of the monasteries") and [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII")'s order that the university disband the [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law") that governed the university's faculty[[25]](#cite_note-26) and stop teaching [scholastic philosophy](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism"). In response, colleges changed their curricula from canon law to [classics](/wiki/Classics "Classics"), the Bible, and mathematics.

Nearly a century later, the university found itself at the centre of a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") schism. Many nobles, intellectuals, and also commoners saw the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") as too similar to the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and felt that it was being used by [The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") to usurp the counties' rightful powers. [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia") emerged as the centre of what ultimately became the [Puritan movement](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans"). In Cambridge, the Puritan movement was particularly strong at [Emmanuel](/wiki/Emmanuel_College,_Cambridge "Emmanuel College, Cambridge"), [St Catharine Hall](/wiki/St_Catharine%27s_College,_Cambridge "St Catharine's College, Cambridge"), [Sidney Sussex](/wiki/Sidney_Sussex_College "Sidney Sussex College"), and [Christ's](/wiki/Christ%27s_College,_Cambridge "Christ's College, Cambridge").[[26]](#cite_note-27) These colleges produced many [nonconformist](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") graduates who greatly influenced, by social position or preaching, some 20,000 Puritans who ultimately left England for [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") and especially the [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") during the [Great Migration](/wiki/Puritan_migration_to_New_England_(1620%E2%80%931640) "Puritan migration to New England (1620–1640)") decade of the 1630s.

### Mathematics and mathematical physics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mathematics and mathematical physics")]

See also: [Lucasian Professor of Mathematics](/wiki/Lucasian_Professor_of_Mathematics "Lucasian Professor of Mathematics") and [Mathematical Tripos](/wiki/Mathematical_Tripos "Mathematical Tripos")

The university quickly established itself as a global leader in the study of mathematics. The university's examination in mathematics, known as the [Mathematical Tripos](/wiki/Mathematical_Tripos "Mathematical Tripos"),[[27]](#cite_note-28) was initially compulsory for all undergraduates studying for the Bachelor of Arts degree, the most common degree first offered at Cambridge. From the time of [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") in the late 17th century until the mid-19th century, the university maintained an especially strong emphasis on [applied mathematics](/wiki/Applied_mathematics "Applied mathematics"), and especially [mathematical physics](/wiki/Mathematical_physics "Mathematical physics"). Students awarded [first class honours](/wiki/British_undergraduate_degree_classification "British undergraduate degree classification") after completing the mathematics Tripos exam are called [wranglers](/wiki/Wrangler_(University_of_Cambridge) "Wrangler (University of Cambridge)"), and the top student among them is known as the [Senior Wrangler](/wiki/Senior_Wrangler "Senior Wrangler"), a position that has been described as "the greatest intellectual achievement attainable in Britain."[[28]](#cite_note-Forfar_1996-29)

The Cambridge Mathematical Tripos is highly competitive and has helped produce some of the most famous names in British science, including [James Clerk Maxwell](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell "James Clerk Maxwell"), [Lord Kelvin](/wiki/William_Thomson,_1st_Baron_Kelvin "William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin"), and [Lord Rayleigh](/wiki/John_William_Strutt,_3rd_Baron_Rayleigh "John William Strutt, 3rd Baron Rayleigh").[[29]](#cite_note-30) However, some famous students, such as [G. H. Hardy](/wiki/G._H._Hardy "G. H. Hardy"), disliked the Tripos system, feeling that students were becoming too focused on accumulating high exam marks at the expense of the subject itself.

Pure mathematics at the University of Cambridge in the 19th century achieved great things, though it largely missed out on substantial developments in French and German mathematics. By the early 20th century, however, pure mathematical research at Cambridge reached the highest international standard, thanks largely to G. H. Hardy and his collaborators, [J. E. Littlewood](/wiki/J._E._Littlewood "J. E. Littlewood") and [Srinivasa Ramanujan](/wiki/Srinivasa_Ramanujan "Srinivasa Ramanujan"). [W. V. D. Hodge](/wiki/W._V._D._Hodge "W. V. D. Hodge") and others helped establish Cambridge as a global leader in [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry") in the 1930s.

### Modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern period")]

See also: [Cambridge movement (philosophy)](/wiki/Cambridge_movement_(philosophy) "Cambridge movement (philosophy)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Selwyn_College_Old_Court%2C_Cambridge%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg/250px-Selwyn_College_Old_Court%2C_Cambridge%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Selwyn_College_Old_Court,_Cambridge,_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)

[Selwyn College](/wiki/Selwyn_College,_Cambridge "Selwyn College, Cambridge"), founded in 1882

The [Cambridge University Act 1856](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Act_1856 "Cambridge University Act 1856") formalised the university's organisational structure and introduced the study of many new subjects, including theology, history, and [Modern languages](/wiki/Modern_language "Modern language").[[30]](#cite_note-31) Resources necessary for new courses in the arts, architecture, and [archaeology](/wiki/Archeology "Archeology") were donated by [Viscount Fitzwilliam](/wiki/Richard_FitzWilliam,_7th_Viscount_FitzWilliam "Richard FitzWilliam, 7th Viscount FitzWilliam") of [Trinity College](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Cambridge "Trinity College, Cambridge"), who also founded [Fitzwilliam Museum](/wiki/Fitzwilliam_Museum "Fitzwilliam Museum") in 1816.[[31]](#cite_note-32) In 1847, [Prince Albert](/wiki/Albert,_Prince_Consort "Albert, Prince Consort") was elected the university's chancellor in a close contest with the [Earl of Powis](/wiki/Earl_of_Powis "Earl of Powis"). As chancellor, Albert reformed university curricula beyond its initial focus on mathematics and classics, adding [modern era history](/wiki/Modern_era "Modern era") and the [natural sciences](/wiki/Natural_science "Natural science"). Between 1896 and 1902, [Downing College](/wiki/Downing_College,_Cambridge "Downing College, Cambridge") sold part of its land to permit the construction of [Downing Site](/wiki/Downing_Site "Downing Site"), the university's grouping of scientific laboratories for the study of [anatomy](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy"), [genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics"), and [Earth sciences](/wiki/Department_of_Earth_Sciences,_University_of_Cambridge "Department of Earth Sciences, University of Cambridge").[[32]](#cite_note-33) During this period, the [New Museums Site](/wiki/New_Museums_Site "New Museums Site") was erected, including the [Cavendish Laboratory](/wiki/Cavendish_Laboratory "Cavendish Laboratory"), which has since moved to [West Cambridge](/wiki/West_Cambridge "West Cambridge"), and other [departments for chemistry](/wiki/Yusuf_Hamied_Department_of_Chemistry "Yusuf Hamied Department of Chemistry") and medicine.[[33]](#cite_note-34) The University of Cambridge began to award PhD degrees in the first third of the 20th century; the first Cambridge PhD in mathematics was awarded in 1924.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

The university contributed significantly to the [Allies' forces](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") with 13,878 members of the university serving and 2,470 being [killed in action](/wiki/Killed_in_action "Killed in action") during the war. Teaching, and the fees it earned, nearly came to a halt during World War I, and severe financial difficulties followed. As a result, the university received its first systematic state support in 1919, and a [Royal commission](/wiki/Royal_commission "Royal commission") was appointed in 1920 to recommend that the university (but not its colleges) begin receiving an annual grant.[[35]](#cite_note-36) Following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the university experienced a rapid expansion in applications and enrolment, partly due to the success and popularity gained by many Cambridge scientists.[[36]](#cite_note-37) This was not without controversies, however. For example, Cambridge researchers were accused in 2023 of helping to develop weapon systems for Iran.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

#### Parliamentary representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Parliamentary representation")]

Main article: [Cambridge University (UK Parliament constituency)](/wiki/Cambridge_University_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Cambridge University (UK Parliament constituency)")

See also: [Category:Members of the Parliament of the United Kingdom for the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Category:Members_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom_for_the_University_of_Cambridge "Category:Members of the Parliament of the United Kingdom for the University of Cambridge")

The University of Cambridge was one of only two universities to hold parliamentary seats in the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") and was later one of 19 represented in the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"). The constituency was created by a [Royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") of 1603 and returned two members of parliament until 1950 when it was abolished by the [Representation of the People Act 1948](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948 "Representation of the People Act 1948"). The constituency was not a geographical area; rather, its electorate consisted of university graduates. Before 1918, the franchise was restricted to male graduates with a [doctorate](/wiki/Doctor_of_Philosophy "Doctor of Philosophy") or [MA](/wiki/Master_of_Arts_(Oxbridge_and_Dublin) "Master of Arts (Oxbridge and Dublin)") degree.

### Women's education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Women's education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Cambridge_Newnham.JPG/250px-Cambridge_Newnham.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cambridge_Newnham.JPG)

[Newnham College](/wiki/Newnham_College,_Cambridge "Newnham College, Cambridge"), one of two colleges at the university which only admit female students

For the first several centuries of its existence, as was the case broadly in England and the world, the University of Cambridge was only open to male students. The first colleges established for women were [Girton College](/wiki/Girton_College,_Cambridge "Girton College, Cambridge"), founded by [Emily Davies](/wiki/Emily_Davies "Emily Davies") in 1869, [Newnham College](/wiki/Newnham_College,_Cambridge "Newnham College, Cambridge"), founded by [Anne Clough](/wiki/Anne_Clough "Anne Clough") and [Henry Sidgwick](/wiki/Henry_Sidgwick "Henry Sidgwick") in 1872, [Hughes Hall](/wiki/Hughes_Hall,_Cambridge "Hughes Hall, Cambridge"), founded in 1885 by [Elizabeth Phillips Hughes](/wiki/Elizabeth_Phillips_Hughes "Elizabeth Phillips Hughes") as the Cambridge Teaching College for Women, [Murray Edwards College](/wiki/Murray_Edwards_College "Murray Edwards College"), founded in 1954 by [Rosemary Murray](/wiki/Rosemary_Murray "Rosemary Murray") as [New Hall](/wiki/Murray_Edwards_College,_Cambridge "Murray Edwards College, Cambridge"), and [Lucy Cavendish College](/wiki/Lucy_Cavendish_College,_Cambridge "Lucy Cavendish College, Cambridge"), founded in 1965. Prior to ultimately being permitted admission to the university in 1948, female students were granted the right to take University of Cambridge exams beginning in the late 19th century.[[38]](#cite_note-39) Women were also allowed to study courses, take examinations, and have prior exam results recorded retroactively, dating back to 1881; for a brief period after the turn of the 20th century, this allowed the [steamboat ladies](/wiki/Steamboat_ladies "Steamboat ladies") to receive *[ad eundem](/wiki/Ad_eundem_gradum "Ad eundem gradum")* degrees from the [University of Dublin](/wiki/University_of_Dublin "University of Dublin").[[39]](#cite_note-40) In 1998, a special graduation ceremony was held in which the women who attended Cambridge before admission was allowed in 1948 were finally conferred their degrees.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Beginning in 1921, women were awarded diplomas that conferred the title associated with the Bachelor of Arts degree. But since women were not yet admitted to the Bachelor of Arts degree program, they were excluded from the university's governance structure. Since University of Cambridge students must belong to a college, and since established colleges remained closed to women, women found admissions restricted to the few university colleges that had been established only for them. [Darwin College](/wiki/Darwin_College,_Cambridge "Darwin College, Cambridge"), the first graduate college of the university, matriculated both male and female students from its inception in 1964 and elected a mixed fellowship. Undergraduate colleges, starting with [Churchill](/wiki/Churchill_College,_Cambridge "Churchill College, Cambridge"), [Clare](/wiki/Clare_College,_Cambridge "Clare College, Cambridge"), and [King](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge")'s colleges, began admitting women between 1972 and 1988. Among women's colleges at the university, [Girton](/wiki/Girton_College,_Cambridge "Girton College, Cambridge") began admitting male students in 1979, and [Lucy Cavendish](/wiki/Lucy_Cavendish_College,_Cambridge "Lucy Cavendish College, Cambridge") began admitting men in 2021. But the other female-only colleges have remained female-only colleges as of 2023. As a result of [St Hilda's College, Oxford](/wiki/St_Hilda%27s_College,_Oxford "St Hilda's College, Oxford"), ending its ban on male students in 2008, Cambridge is now the only remaining university in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") with female-only colleges; [Newnham](/wiki/Newnham_College,_Cambridge "Newnham College, Cambridge") and [Murray Edwards](/wiki/Murray_Edwards_College,_Cambridge "Murray Edwards College, Cambridge") only admit female students.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) As of the 2019–2020 academic year, the university's male to female enrolment, including post-graduates, was nearly balanced with its total student population being 53% male and 47% female.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

In 2018 and later years, the university has come under some criticism and faced legal challenges over alleged sexual harassment at the university.[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46) In 2019, for example, former student Danielle Bradford, represented by sexual harassment lawyer [Ann Olivarius](/wiki/Ann_Olivarius "Ann Olivarius"), sued the university for its handling of her sexual misconduct complaint. "I was told that I should think about it very carefully because making a complaint could affect my place in my department", Bradford alleged in 2019.[[46]](#cite_note-47) In 2020, hundreds of current and former students accused the university in a letter, citing "a complete failure" to deal with sexual misconduct complaints.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### Town and gown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Town and gown")]

Main article: [Town and gown](/wiki/Town_and_gown "Town and gown")

Further information: [Away with the learning of clerks, away with it!](/wiki/Away_with_the_learning_of_clerks,_away_with_it! "Away with the learning of clerks, away with it!") and [Silicon Fen](/wiki/Silicon_Fen "Silicon Fen")

The relationship between the university and the city of [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge") has sometimes been uneasy. The phrase [town and gown](/wiki/Town_and_gown "Town and gown") continues to be employed to distinguish between Cambridge residents (town) and University of Cambridge students (gown), who historically wore [academical dress](/wiki/Academic_dress "Academic dress"). Ferocious rivalry between Cambridge's residents and university students have periodically erupted over the centuries. During the [Peasants' Revolt](/wiki/Peasants%27_Revolt "Peasants' Revolt") of 1381, strong clashes led to attacks and [looting](/wiki/Looting "Looting") of university properties as locals contested the privileges granted by the British government to the University of Cambridge's academic staff. Residents burned university property in [Market Square](/wiki/Market_Hill,_Cambridge "Market Hill, Cambridge") to the famed rallying cry "[Away with the learning of clerks, away with it!](/wiki/Away_with_the_learning_of_clerks,_away_with_it! "Away with the learning of clerks, away with it!")".[[48]](#cite_note-49) Following these events, the [University of Cambridge's Chancellor](/wiki/List_of_chancellors_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "List of chancellors of the University of Cambridge") was given special powers allowing him to prosecute criminals and reestablish order in the city. Attempts at reconciliation between the city's residents and students followed; in the 16th century, agreements were signed to improve the quality of streets and student accommodation around the city. However, this was followed by new confrontations when the [plague](/wiki/Plague_(disease) "Plague (disease)") reached Cambridge in 1630 and colleges refused to assist those affected by the disease by locking their sites.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Such conflicts between Cambridge's residents and university students have largely disappeared since the 16th century, and the university has grown as a source of enormous employment and expanded wealth in Cambridge and the region.[[50]](#cite_note-51) The university also has proven a source of extraordinary growth in [high tech](/wiki/High_tech "High tech") and [biotech](/wiki/Biotech "Biotech") start-ups and established companies and associated providers of services to these companies. The economic growth associated with the university's high tech and biotech growth has been labeled the Cambridge Phenomenon, and has included the addition of 1,500 new companies and as many as 40,000 new jobs added between 1960 and 2010, mostly at [Silicon Fen](/wiki/Silicon_Fen "Silicon Fen"), a business cluster launched by the university in the late 20th century.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

### Myths, legends and traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Myths, legends and traditions")]

Main article: [University of Cambridge legends](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge_legends "University of Cambridge legends")

See also: [Category:Terminology of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Category:Terminology_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Category:Terminology of the University of Cambridge")

Further information: [Nine Lessons and Carols](/wiki/Nine_Lessons_and_Carols "Nine Lessons and Carols") and [wooden spoon (award)](/wiki/Wooden_spoon_(award) "Wooden spoon (award)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Queens%27_College_-_Mathematical_Bridge.jpg/250px-Queens%27_College_-_Mathematical_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queens%27_College_-_Mathematical_Bridge.jpg)

[Mathematical Bridge](/wiki/Mathematical_Bridge "Mathematical Bridge") crossing [River Cam](/wiki/River_Cam "River Cam") at [Queens' College](/wiki/Queens%27_College,_Cambridge "Queens' College, Cambridge")

Partly because of the University of Cambridge's extensive history, which now exceeds 800 years, the university has developed a large number of traditions, myths, and legends. Some are true, some are not, and some were true but have been discontinued but have been propagated nonetheless by generations of students and tour guides.

One such discontinued tradition is that of the [wooden spoon](/wiki/Wooden_spoon_(award) "Wooden spoon (award)"), the prize awarded to the student with the lowest passing honours grade in the final examinations of the university's [Mathematical Tripos](/wiki/Mathematical_Tripos "Mathematical Tripos"). The last of these spoons was awarded in 1909 to Cuthbert Lempriere Holthouse, an oarsman of the Lady Margaret Boat Club at [St John's College](/wiki/St_John%27s_College,_Cambridge "St John's College, Cambridge"). It was more than one metre in length and had an oar blade for a handle. It can now be seen outside the Senior Combination Room of St John's College. Since 1908, examination results have been published alphabetically within class rather than in strict order of merit, which made it difficult to ascertain the student with the lowest passing grade deserving of the spoon, leading to discontinuation of the tradition.

Each Christmas Eve, [The Festival of Nine Lessons and Carols](/wiki/Nine_Lessons_and_Carols "Nine Lessons and Carols"), sung by the [Choir of King's College](/wiki/Choir_of_King%27s_College,_Cambridge "Choir of King's College, Cambridge"), are broadcast globally on [BBC World Service](/wiki/BBC_World_Service "BBC World Service") television and radio and syndicated to hundreds of additional radio stations in the U.S. and elsewhere. The radio broadcast has been a national Christmas Eve tradition since 1928, though the festival has existed since 1918 and the celebration itself originated even earlier at [Truro Cathedral](/wiki/Truro_Cathedral "Truro Cathedral") in Cornwall in 1880.[[52]](#cite_note-guardian-truro-53) The first television broadcast of the festival was in 1954.[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55)

### Buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Buildings")]

See also: [A Classical Adventure: The Architectural History of Downing College, Cambridge](/wiki/A_Classical_Adventure:_The_Architectural_History_of_Downing_College,_Cambridge "A Classical Adventure: The Architectural History of Downing College, Cambridge"); [Cambridge Medical School building](/wiki/Cambridge_Medical_School_building "Cambridge Medical School building"); [Cambridge Observatory](/wiki/Cambridge_Observatory "Cambridge Observatory"); [Cambridge University Library](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Library "Cambridge University Library"); [Cambridge University Real Tennis Club](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Real_Tennis_Club "Cambridge University Real Tennis Club"); [Fitzwilliam Museum](/wiki/Fitzwilliam_Museum "Fitzwilliam Museum"); [Goldie Boathouse](/wiki/Goldie_Boathouse "Goldie Boathouse"); [Lady Mitchell Hall](/wiki/Lady_Mitchell_Hall "Lady Mitchell Hall"); [Old Schools](/wiki/Old_Schools "Old Schools"); [Senate House, Cambridge](/wiki/Senate_House,_Cambridge "Senate House, Cambridge"); [Scott Polar Research Institute](/wiki/Scott_Polar_Research_Institute "Scott Polar Research Institute"); [University of Cambridge Sports Centre](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge_Sports_Centre "University of Cambridge Sports Centre"); and [William Gates Building, Cambridge](/wiki/William_Gates_Building,_Cambridge "William Gates Building, Cambridge")

The university occupies a central location within the city of [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge"). University of Cambridge students represent approximately 20 percent of the town's population, which was 145,674 as of 2021, resulting in a lower age demographic in the city.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

Most of the university's older colleges are located near the city centre, through which [River Cam](/wiki/River_Cam "River Cam") flows. Students and others traditionally [punt](/wiki/Punt_(boat) "Punt (boat)") on the River Cam, which provides views of the university's buildings that surround the river.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

A few of the notable University of Cambridge buildings are [King's College Chapel](/wiki/King%27s_College_Chapel,_Cambridge "King's College Chapel, Cambridge");[[57]](#cite_note-58) the history faculty building[[58]](#cite_note-59) designed by [James Stirling](/wiki/James_Stirling_(architect) "James Stirling (architect)"); and the New Court and Cripps Buildings at [St John's College](/wiki/St_John%27s_College,_Cambridge "St John's College, Cambridge").[[59]](#cite_note-60) The [brickwork](/wiki/Brickwork "Brickwork") of several colleges is notable: [Queens' College](/wiki/Queens%27_College,_Cambridge "Queens' College, Cambridge") has some of the earliest patterned brickwork in England[[60]](#cite_note-61) and the brick walls of St John's College are examples of [English bond](/wiki/English_bond "English bond"), [Flemish bond](/wiki/Flemish_bond "Flemish bond"), and [Running bond](/wiki/Running_bond "Running bond").[[61]](#cite_note-62)

### Sites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Sites")]

The university is divided into several sites, which house the university's various departments, including:[[62]](#cite_note-63)

* [Addenbrooke's Hospital](/wiki/Addenbrooke%27s_Hospital "Addenbrooke's Hospital")
* [Downing Site](/wiki/Downing_Site "Downing Site")
* Madingley/Girton
* [New Museums Site](/wiki/New_Museums_Site "New Museums Site")
* [North West Cambridge Development](/wiki/North_West_Cambridge_development "North West Cambridge development")
* [Old Addenbrooke's Site](/wiki/Old_Addenbrooke%27s_Site "Old Addenbrooke's Site")
* [Old Schools](/wiki/Old_Schools "Old Schools")
* [Sidgwick Site](/wiki/Sidgwick_Site "Sidgwick Site")
* [Silver Street](/wiki/Silver_Street,_Cambridge "Silver Street, Cambridge")/[Mill Lane](/wiki/Pembroke_Street,_Cambridge "Pembroke Street, Cambridge")
* [West Cambridge](/wiki/West_Cambridge "West Cambridge")

The university's [School of Clinical Medicine](/wiki/School_of_Clinical_Medicine,_University_of_Cambridge "School of Clinical Medicine, University of Cambridge") is based in [Addenbrooke's Hospital](/wiki/Addenbrooke%27s_Hospital "Addenbrooke's Hospital"), where medical students undergo their three-year clinical placement period after obtaining their [BA](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") degree.[[63]](#cite_note-64) The [West Cambridge](/wiki/West_Cambridge "West Cambridge") site is undergoing a major expansion and will host new buildings and fields for university sports.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Since 1990, [Cambridge Judge Business School](/wiki/Cambridge_Judge_Business_School "Cambridge Judge Business School"), on [Trumpington Street](/wiki/Trumpington_Street "Trumpington Street"), provides management education courses and is consistently ranked among the top 20 business schools in the world by *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")*.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

Many of the sites are quite close together, and the area around Cambridge is reasonably flat. Furthermore, students are not permitted to hold car park permits except under special circumstances. For these reasons, the favourite mode of transport for students is the bicycle; an estimated one-fifth of journeys in the city are made by bike.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

## Organisation and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Organisation and administration")]

See also: [Colleges of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Colleges_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Colleges of the University of Cambridge") and [Schools of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Schools_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Schools of the University of Cambridge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Cam_colls_from_johns.jpg/250px-Cam_colls_from_johns.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cam_colls_from_johns.jpg)

View over [Trinity College](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Cambridge "Trinity College, Cambridge"), [Gonville and Caius College](/wiki/Gonville_and_Caius_College,_Cambridge "Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge"), [Trinity Hall](/wiki/Trinity_Hall,_Cambridge "Trinity Hall, Cambridge"), and [Clare College](/wiki/Clare_College,_Cambridge "Clare College, Cambridge") towards [King's College](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge") Chapel seen from [St John's College](/wiki/St_John%27s_College,_Cambridge "St John's College, Cambridge") Chapel on the left. In front of King's College Chapel is the [Senate House](/wiki/Senate_House_(University_of_Cambridge) "Senate House (University of Cambridge)").

Cambridge is a [collegiate university](/wiki/Collegiate_university "Collegiate university"), which means that its colleges are self-governing and independent, each with its own property, endowments, and income. Most colleges bring together academics and students from a broad range of disciplines. Each faculty, school, or department at the university includes academics affiliated with differing colleges. The university is legally structured as an [exempt charity](/wiki/Exempt_charity "Exempt charity") and a common law corporation. Its corporate titles include the [Chancellor](/wiki/List_of_chancellors_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "List of chancellors of the University of Cambridge"), Masters, and Scholars of the University of Cambridge.[[4]](#cite_note-fullname-4)

The college faculties are responsible for giving lectures, arranging seminars, performing research, and determining the [syllabi](/wiki/Syllabus "Syllabus") for teaching, all of which is overseen by the university's general board. Together with the central administration headed by the [Vice-Chancellor](/wiki/List_of_vice-chancellors_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "List of vice-chancellors of the University of Cambridge"), the college faculties make up the University of Cambridge. Facilities such as libraries are provided on all these levels by the university. The [Cambridge University Library](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Library "Cambridge University Library") is the university's largest and primary library. [Squire Law Library](/wiki/Faculty_of_Law,_University_of_Cambridge "Faculty of Law, University of Cambridge") is the primary library for the university's [students of law](/wiki/Faculty_of_Law,_University_of_Cambridge "Faculty of Law, University of Cambridge"). Individual colleges each maintain a multi-discipline library designed for each college's respective undergraduates. College libraries tend to operate 24/7 and their usage in generally restricted to members of the college. Conversely, libraries operated by departments are generally open to all students of the university, regardless of subject.

### Colleges

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Bridge_of_Sighs%2C_St_John%27s_College%2C_Cambridge%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg/250px-Bridge_of_Sighs%2C_St_John%27s_College%2C_Cambridge%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bridge_of_Sighs,_St_John%27s_College,_Cambridge,_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)

The colleges are self-governing institutions with their own endowments and property, each founded as components of the university. All students and most academics are attached to a college. The colleges' importance lies in the housing, welfare, social functions, and undergraduate teaching they provide. All faculties, departments, research centres, and laboratories belong to the university, which arranges lectures and awards degrees, but undergraduates receive their overall academic supervision within the colleges through small group teaching sessions, which often include just one student; though in many cases students go to other colleges for supervision if the teaching fellows at their college do not specialise in a student's particular area of academic focus. Each college appoints its own teaching staff and fellows, both of whom are members of a university department. The colleges also decide which undergraduates to admit to the university, in accordance with university standards and regulations. Costs to students for room and board vary considerably from college to college.[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69) Similarly, the investment in student education by each college at the university varies widely between the colleges.[[69]](#cite_note-TCS_college_spending-70)

Cambridge has 31 colleges, two of which, [Murray Edwards](/wiki/Murray_Edwards_College,_Cambridge "Murray Edwards College, Cambridge") and [Newnham](/wiki/Newnham_College,_Cambridge "Newnham College, Cambridge"), only admit women. The other colleges are [mixed](/wiki/Mixed-sex_education "Mixed-sex education"). [Darwin](/wiki/Darwin_College,_Cambridge "Darwin College, Cambridge") was the first college to admit both men and women. In 1972, [Churchill](/wiki/Churchill_College,_Cambridge "Churchill College, Cambridge"), [Clare](/wiki/Clare_College,_Cambridge "Clare College, Cambridge"), and [King's](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge") were the first previously all-male colleges to admit female undergraduates. In 1988, [Magdalene](/wiki/Magdalene_College,_Cambridge "Magdalene College, Cambridge") became the last all-male college to accept women.[[70]](#cite_note-71) [Clare Hall](/wiki/Clare_Hall,_Cambridge "Clare Hall, Cambridge") and Darwin only admit postgraduates, and [Hughes Hall](/wiki/Hughes_Hall,_Cambridge "Hughes Hall, Cambridge"), [St Edmund's](/wiki/St_Edmund%27s_College,_Cambridge "St Edmund's College, Cambridge"), and [Wolfson](/wiki/Wolfson_College,_Cambridge "Wolfson College, Cambridge") only admit [mature undergraduate and graduate students](/wiki/Adult_learner "Adult learner") who are 21 years old or older on the date of their [matriculation](/wiki/Matriculation "Matriculation"). [Lucy Cavendish](/wiki/Lucy_Cavendish_College,_Cambridge "Lucy Cavendish College, Cambridge"), which was previously a women-only mature college, began admitting both men and women in 2021.[[71]](#cite_note-72) All other colleges admit both undergraduate and postgraduate students without any age restrictions.

Colleges are not required to admit students in all subjects; some colleges choose not to offer subjects such as architecture, art history, or theology, but most offer a complete range of academic specialties and related courses. Some colleges maintain a relative strength and associated reputation for expertise in certain academic disciplines. Churchill, for example, has a reputation for its expertise and focus on sciences and engineering, in part due to the requirement imposed by [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") upon the college's founding that 70% of its students studied mathematics, engineering, and the sciences.[[72]](#cite_note-73) Other colleges have more informal academic focus and even demonstrate ideological focus, such as [King's](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge"), which is known for its left-wing political orientation,[[73]](#cite_note-74) and Churchill and [Robinson](/wiki/Robinson_College,_Cambridge "Robinson College, Cambridge"), both of which have a reputation for academic focus on [sustainability](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") and environmentalism.[[74]](#cite_note-75) Three theological colleges at the university, [Westcott House](/wiki/Westcott_House,_Cambridge "Westcott House, Cambridge"), [Westminster College](/wiki/Westminster_College_(Cambridge) "Westminster College (Cambridge)"), and [Ridley Hall](/wiki/Ridley_Hall,_Cambridge "Ridley Hall, Cambridge"), are members of the [Cambridge Theological Federation](/wiki/Cambridge_Theological_Federation "Cambridge Theological Federation") and associated in partnership with the university.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

### Schools, faculties and departments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Schools, faculties and departments")]

Main article: [Schools of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Schools_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Schools of the University of Cambridge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Across_the_lake_to_Madingley_Hall_%28geograph_6402209_by_John_Sutton%29.jpg/250px-Across_the_lake_to_Madingley_Hall_%28geograph_6402209_by_John_Sutton%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Across_the_lake_to_Madingley_Hall_(geograph_6402209_by_John_Sutton).jpg)

[Institute of Continuing Education](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge_Institute_of_Continuing_Education "University of Cambridge Institute of Continuing Education") at Madingley Hall

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Old_Schools_from_Kings_Parade%2C_Cambridge%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg/250px-Old_Schools_from_Kings_Parade%2C_Cambridge%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Schools_from_Kings_Parade,_Cambridge,_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)

[Old Schools](/wiki/Old_Schools "Old Schools") (left) houses the university's administrative offices.

In addition to the 31 colleges, the university maintains more than 150 departments, faculties, schools, syndicates, and other academic institutions.[[77]](#cite_note-78) Members of these are usually members of one of the colleges, and responsibility for the entire academic programme of the university is divided among them.

The university has a department dedicated to providing [continuing education](/wiki/Continuing_education "Continuing education"), the [Institute of Continuing Education](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge_Institute_of_Continuing_Education "University of Cambridge Institute of Continuing Education"), which is based primarily in [Madingley Hall](/wiki/Madingley_Hall "Madingley Hall"), a 16th-century manor house in [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire"). Its award-bearing programmes include both undergraduate certificates and part-time master's degrees.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

A school in the University of Cambridge is a broad administrative grouping of related faculties and other units. Each has an elected supervisory body known as a Council, composed of representatives of the various constituent bodies. The University of Cambridge maintains six such schools:[[79]](#cite_note-80)

* Arts and Humanities
* Biological Sciences
* Clinical Medicine
* Humanities and Social Sciences
* Physical Sciences
* Technology

Teaching and research at the university is organised by faculties. The faculties have varying organisational substructures that partly reflect their respective histories and the university's operational needs, which may include a number of departments and other institutions. A small number of bodies called syndicates hold responsibility for teaching and research, including for the [University of Cambridge Local Examinations Syndicate](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge_Local_Examinations_Syndicate "University of Cambridge Local Examinations Syndicate"), the [University Press](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Press "Cambridge University Press"), and the [University Library](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Library "Cambridge University Library").

### Central administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Central administration")]

#### Chancellor and vice-chancellor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Chancellor and vice-chancellor")]

See also: [List of chancellors of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/List_of_chancellors_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "List of chancellors of the University of Cambridge") and [List of vice-chancellors of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/List_of_vice-chancellors_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "List of vice-chancellors of the University of Cambridge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Cmglee_Cambridge_graduation_officers.jpg/250px-Cmglee_Cambridge_graduation_officers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cmglee_Cambridge_graduation_officers.jpg)

[Regent House](/wiki/Regent_House "Regent House") officers following a July 2014 graduation ceremony

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/DivinityFaculty.jpg/250px-DivinityFaculty.jpg)](/wiki/File:DivinityFaculty.jpg)

[Faculty of Divinity](/wiki/Faculty_of_Divinity,_University_of_Cambridge "Faculty of Divinity, University of Cambridge") at the university

The [chancellor](/wiki/List_of_chancellors_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "List of chancellors of the University of Cambridge") of the university is a ten-year position that is mainly ceremonial and is held currently by [Chris Smith, Baron Smith of Finsbury](/wiki/Chris_Smith,_Baron_Smith_of_Finsbury "Chris Smith, Baron Smith of Finsbury"), who succeeded [David Sainsbury, Baron Sainsbury of Turville](/wiki/David_Sainsbury,_Baron_Sainsbury_of_Turville "David Sainsbury, Baron Sainsbury of Turville") in 2025.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

The current [vice-chancellor](/wiki/List_of_vice-chancellors_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "List of vice-chancellors of the University of Cambridge") is [Deborah Prentice](/wiki/Deborah_Prentice "Deborah Prentice"), who began her role in July 2023.[[81]](#cite_note-82) While the chancellor's office is ceremonial, the vice-chancellor serves as the university's *de facto* principal administrative officer. The university's internal governance is carried out almost entirely by [Regent House](/wiki/Regent_House "Regent House"), augmented by some external representation from the audit committee and four external members of the [university's council](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Council "Cambridge University Council").[[82]](#cite_note-83)

#### Senate and the Regent House

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Senate and the Regent House")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/CamLight.jpg/250px-CamLight.jpg)](/wiki/File:CamLight.jpg)

Light show on [Senate House](/wiki/Senate_House,_Cambridge "Senate House, Cambridge") for the 800th anniversary of the university's founding

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/20130808_Castle_End_from_Kings_Bridge.jpg/250px-20130808_Castle_End_from_Kings_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:20130808_Castle_End_from_Kings_Bridge.jpg)

Old Court at [Clare College](/wiki/Clare_College,_Cambridge "Clare College, Cambridge")

The university senate consists of all holders of the [MA](/wiki/Master_of_Arts_(Oxbridge_and_Dublin) "Master of Arts (Oxbridge and Dublin)") or higher degrees and is responsible for electing the chancellor and the high steward. Until 1950, when the [Cambridge University constituency](/wiki/Cambridge_University_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Cambridge University (UK Parliament constituency)") was abolished, it was also responsible for electing two members of the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"). Prior to 1926, the university senate was the university's governing body, fulfilling the functions that [Regent House](/wiki/Regent_House "Regent House") has provided since.[[83]](#cite_note-84) Regent House is the university's governing body, comprising all resident senior members of the university and the colleges, the chancellor, the [high steward](/wiki/High_Steward_(academia) "High Steward (academia)"), the deputy high steward, and the commissary.[[84]](#cite_note-85) Public representatives of Regent House are the two [proctors](/wiki/Proctor "Proctor"), elected to serve for one year terms upon their nominations by the colleges.

#### Council and general board

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Council and general board")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Senate_House_Passage_in_the_snow.JPG/250px-Senate_House_Passage_in_the_snow.JPG)](/wiki/File:Senate_House_Passage_in_the_snow.JPG)

[Senate House Passage](/wiki/Trinity_Lane "Trinity Lane") in the snow with [Senate House](/wiki/Senate_House,_Cambridge "Senate House, Cambridge") on the right and [Gonville and Caius College](/wiki/Gonville_and_Caius_College,_Cambridge "Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge") on the left

Although [Cambridge University Council](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Council "Cambridge University Council") is the university's principal executive and policy-making body, the council reports to, and is held accountable by, [Regent House](/wiki/Regent_House "Regent House") through a variety of checks and balances. The council is obliged to advise Regent House on matters of general concern to the university, which it does by publishing notices to the *[Cambridge University Reporter](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Reporter "Cambridge University Reporter")*, the university's official journal.[[85]](#cite_note-86) In March 2008, Regent House voted to increase from two to four the number of external members on the council,[[86]](#cite_note-87)[[87]](#cite_note-88) and this was approved by Her Majesty the Queen in July 2008.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

The general board of the faculties is responsible for the university's academic and educational policies[[89]](#cite_note-90) and is accountable to the council for its management of these affairs. Faculty boards are accountable to the general board; other boards and syndicates are accountable either to the general board or to the council. Under this organisational structure, the university's various arms are kept under supervision of both the central administration and Regent House.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

### Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Finances")]

#### Endowment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Endowment")]

The Cambridge University Endowment Fund is the main vehicle of investment for the university.[[91]](#cite_note-92) In the fiscal year ending 31 July 2025, the university group, excluding colleges, reported a total endowment of £2.76 billion.[[5]](#cite_note-annualfinstat25-5)

#### Benefactions and fundraising

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Benefactions and fundraising")]

In the fiscal year ending 31 July 2025, the central university, excluding colleges, reported total group income of £2.66 billion, of which £605 million was from research grants and contracts.[[5]](#cite_note-annualfinstat25-5) In July 2022, the *Dear World, Yours Cambridge* Campaign for the university and colleges concluded, raising a total of £2.217 billion in commitments.[[92]](#cite_note-finstat23-93)

The university maintains multiple scholarship programs. The Stormzy Scholarship for Black UK Students covers tuition costs for two students and maintenance grants for up to four years.[[93]](#cite_note-94) In 2000, [Bill Gates](/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates") of [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") donated US$210 million through the [Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation](/wiki/Bill_and_Melinda_Gates_Foundation "Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation") to endow [Gates Cambridge Scholarships](/wiki/Gates_Cambridge_Scholarship "Gates Cambridge Scholarship") for students from outside the United Kingdom to pursue full-time postgraduate study at Cambridge.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

In October 2021, the university suspended its £400m collaboration with the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"), citing allegations that UAE was involved in illegal hacking of the university's computer and storage systems using [NSO Group](/wiki/NSO_Group "NSO Group")'s [Pegasus](/wiki/Pegasus_(spyware) "Pegasus (spyware)") software. UAE also was behind the leak of more than 50,000 phone numbers, including hundreds belonging to British citizens. [Stephen Toope](/wiki/Stephen_Toope "Stephen Toope"), the university's outgoing Vice-Chancellor, said the decision to suspend its collaboration with UAE also was a result of additional revelations about UAE's Pegasus software hacking.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

#### Bonds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Bonds")]

The University of Cambridge borrowed £350 million in October 2012 by issuing 40-year security [bonds](/wiki/Bond_(finance) "Bond (finance)"),[[96]](#cite_note-CN2012oct11ib-97) whose interest rate is approximately 0.6 percent higher than the [British government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom")'s 40-year bond.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

### Affiliations and memberships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Affiliations and memberships")]

The University of Cambridge is a member of the [Russell Group](/wiki/Russell_Group "Russell Group") of research-led [British universities](/wiki/Universities_in_the_United_Kingdom "Universities in the United Kingdom"), the [G5](/wiki/G5_(education) "G5 (education)"), the [League of European Research Universities](/wiki/League_of_European_Research_Universities "League of European Research Universities"), the [International Alliance of Research Universities](/wiki/International_Alliance_of_Research_Universities "International Alliance of Research Universities"), and it is part of the so-called [golden triangle](/wiki/Golden_triangle_(universities) "Golden triangle (universities)") of [research intensive universities](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in the [south of England](/wiki/South_England "South England").[[98]](#cite_note-99) It is also closely linked to the [high tech](/wiki/High_tech "High tech") business cluster known as [Silicon Fen](/wiki/Silicon_Fen "Silicon Fen") and is part of [Cambridge University Health Partners](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Health_Partners "Cambridge University Health Partners"), Europe's largest [academic health science centre](/wiki/Academic_health_science_centre "Academic health science centre").
