#### Process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Process")]

[![St John's New Court](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/St_John%27s_College_New_Court_1%2C_Cambridge%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg/250px-St_John%27s_College_New_Court_1%2C_Cambridge%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_John%27s_College_New_Court_1,_Cambridge,_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)

[St John's College](/wiki/St_John%27s_College,_Cambridge "St John's College, Cambridge") New Court

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/TrinityCollegeCamGreatCourt.jpg/250px-TrinityCollegeCamGreatCourt.jpg)](/wiki/File:TrinityCollegeCamGreatCourt.jpg)

[Great Court](/wiki/Trinity_Great_Court "Trinity Great Court") of [Trinity College](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Cambridge "Trinity College, Cambridge"), dating back to the 16th Century

Admission to the University of Cambridge is extremely competitive. In 2022, for instance, around 15% of applicants were admitted.[[109]](#cite_note-114)[[110]](#cite_note-115) In 2021, Cambridge introduced an over-subscription clause to its offers of admission, which also permits the university to withdraw acceptances if too many students meet its selective entrance criteria. The clause can be invoked in the event of circumstances outside the reasonable control of the university. The clause was introduced following a record number of [A-level](/wiki/GCE_Advanced_Level "GCE Advanced Level") pupils who obtained the highest grades from teacher assessment, which was introduced due to the cancellation of A-level examinations during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[111]](#cite_note-116)[[112]](#cite_note-117)

The university's standard offer for most courses is set at A\*AA,[[113]](#cite_note-118)[[114]](#cite_note-119) with A\*A\*A for science courses, or equivalent in other examination systems, e.g. 7,6,6 or 7,7,6 in IB. The university is consistently designated as a 'high-tariff' institution by the [Department for Education](/wiki/Department_for_Education "Department for Education"), with the average undergraduate entrant to the university in recent years amassing between 165–166 [UCAS Tariff](/wiki/UCAS_Tariff "UCAS Tariff") points (out of a maximum of 168) in their top three pre-university qualifications – the equivalent of A\*A\*A\* at [A-Level](/wiki/A-level_(United_Kingdom) "A-level (United Kingdom)").[[105]](#cite_note-Mean_Tariff-107) Based on 2022/23 [HESA](/wiki/Higher_Education_Statistics_Agency "Higher Education Statistics Agency") entry standards data published in domestic league tables, which include a broad range of qualifications beyond the top three exam grades, the average student at the University of Cambridge achieved 206 points.[[104]](#cite_note-CUG_Entry-105)

Due to a high proportion of applicants receiving the highest school grades, an interview process was introduced as a component of consideration for admission. Interviews are performed by College Fellows, who evaluate candidates on unexamined factors including potential for [original thinking](/wiki/Critical_thinking "Critical thinking") and creativity.[[115]](#cite_note-intguide-120) Prior to 2020 these interviews were normally held in person but moved online during the COVID-19 pandemic and have, at most colleges, remained online since.[[116]](#cite_note-121) For exceptional candidates, a matriculation offer is sometimes offered, requiring only two A-levels at grade E or above. [Sutton Trust](/wiki/Sutton_Trust "Sutton Trust") maintains that the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") and the University of Cambridge recruit disproportionately from eight schools, which account for 1,310 [Oxbridge](/wiki/Oxbridge "Oxbridge") places over three years contrasted with 1,220 from 2,900 other schools.[[117]](#cite_note-122)

Strong applicants who are not successful in being admitted to their chosen college at the university may be placed in the [Winter Pool](/wiki/Winter_Pool "Winter Pool"), where they can be considered for admission to other university colleges, which maintains consistency throughout the colleges, some of which receive more applicants than others.

Undergraduate applications are processed through [UCAS](/wiki/UCAS "UCAS"), and the deadline for their submission currently is mid-October in the year before prior to beginning. Until the 1980s, candidates for all subjects were required to take special entrance examinations,[[118]](#cite_note-123) which have since been replaced by additional tests for some subjects, such as the Thinking Skills Assessment and Cambridge Law Test.[[119]](#cite_note-124) The university has at times considered reintroducing an admissions exam for all subjects.[[120]](#cite_note-125)

Graduate admission is first decided by the faculty or department responsible for the applicant's respective academic subject. An offer of acceptance effectively guarantees admission to the university, though not necessarily the applicant's preferred choice of college.[[121]](#cite_note-126)

#### Winter pool

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Winter pool")]

The **Winter Pool** or **inter-College Pool** is part of the undergraduate application process intended to ensure that the best applicants are offered admission.[[122]](#cite_note-127) Approximately 20–25% of undergraduate admissions are awarded through the Pool. Each college can place applicants in the Winter Pool. These applicants' applications are then considered by Admissions Tutors or Directors of Studies during the pool, which takes place over three days in January prior to the release of the university's admissions decisions.[[123]](#cite_note-:0a-128)

For each subject, colleges create an ordered list of the pooled applicants they seek to admit, and take turns choosing applicants. Colleges with specific student requirements, such as mature colleges and women-only colleges, are given priority over applicants eligible for their colleges. Some applicants are selected from the pool by the college that originally pooled them.[[123]](#cite_note-:0a-128) Once all the colleges have selected as many applicants as they need, the pool ends. Some applicants are then interviewed a second time by the colleges before final admissions decisions are made.

Colleges can pool any candidate, either because the college has no space but believes the applicant is strong enough to get a place, or because the college wants to compare that applicant to other pooled applicants. Most applicants in the pool are pooled at their original college's discretion, but some candidates meet the compulsory pooling criteria.[[124]](#cite_note-129)

There were, as of the 2020–21 admissions cycle, only two grounds for compulsory pooling. For post-qualified applicants, their achieved grades at A level or equivalent and, for applicants with overseas interviews, an interview score of at least eight is achieved in all interviews. The second criterion does not apply to medicine applicants.[[125]](#cite_note-WhatDoTheyKno.comw-2020-130) Previously, [AS-Level](/wiki/GCE_Advanced_Subsidiary_Level "GCE Advanced Subsidiary Level") UMS have been used as pooling criteria, but after [A-levels](/wiki/GCE_Advanced_Level "GCE Advanced Level") became linear this was discontinued.[[125]](#cite_note-WhatDoTheyKno.comw-2020-130)

Compulsory pooling criteria for post-qualified candidates

| Qualification Type | Minimum grades | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [A-Levels](/wiki/A-Levels "A-Levels") | A\*A\*A\* | For science applicants, at least three A\*s must be in science/maths subjects |
| [International Baccalaureate](/wiki/International_Baccalaureate "International Baccalaureate") | 43 points overall with 776 at higher level or 42 points overall with 777 at higher level | For science applicants, at least two 7s must be in science/maths subjects |
| [Scottish Advanced Highers](/wiki/Advanced_Higher "Advanced Higher") | A1A1A1 | For science applicants, at least 3 A1s must be in science/maths subjects |

As of 2012, there is only one specifically identified category for pooled applicants, which is known as **S**, meaning that the application is in special need of reassessment. This is used when candidates whose initial interview scores are of questionable accuracy, such as when a candidate received very different scores from different interviewers, experienced technical issues with interviews conducted over the internet, or was impacted by significant extenuating circumstances such as illness or the loss of a family member.[[125]](#cite_note-WhatDoTheyKno.comw-2020-130)

Pooled applicants who are fished by a college may be offered a place immediately or may be invited for an interview. In 2020, just 89 applicants were invited for second interviews, 34 of whom received offers of admission.[[123]](#cite_note-:0a-128)

Each year, approximately 3,500 applicants receive offers from their preference college and a further 1,000 to 1,100 applicants are offered admission by another college through the pool. On average, one in five applicants is pooled and around one in four pooled applicants receives an offer of admission.[[123]](#cite_note-:0a-128)

Statistics released by the university show that some colleges regularly receive particularly high numbers of applicants, and these colleges tend to take fewer applicants from the pool. Other colleges regularly draw a greater proportion of their undergraduate intake from the pool.[[126]](#cite_note-131)

#### Access

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Access")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Percentage_of_state-school_students_at_Oxford_and_Cambridge.png/250px-Percentage_of_state-school_students_at_Oxford_and_Cambridge.png)](/wiki/File:Percentage_of_state-school_students_at_Oxford_and_Cambridge.png)

Percent of state school students at Cambridge and [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford")[[127]](#cite_note-132)[[128]](#cite_note-133)

Public debate in the United Kingdom exists over whether admissions processes used at the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") and Cambridge are entirely merit-based and fair, whether enough students from [state schools](/wiki/State_school#United_Kingdom "State school") are encouraged to apply, and whether these students are offered sufficient admission. In 2020–21, 71% of all successful applicants were from [state schools](/wiki/State_school#United_Kingdom "State school")[[129]](#cite_note-134) compared to approximately 93% of all students in the UK who attended state schools and 82% of post-16 students.[[130]](#cite_note-135)

Critics have argued that the relative lower percent of state school applicants with the required grades for admission to Cambridge and Oxford has had a negative impact on Oxford and Cambridge's collective reputation, though both universities have encouraged pupils from state schools to apply to help redress the perceived imbalance.[[131]](#cite_note-136) Others counter that government pressure to increase state school admissions constitutes inappropriate [social engineering](/wiki/Social_engineering_(political_science) "Social engineering (political science)").[[132]](#cite_note-137)[[133]](#cite_note-138) The proportion of undergraduates drawn from independent schools has dropped over the years, constituting, as of 2020, 26% of total admissions among the university's 3,436 applicants from independent schools compared to 23% of the 9,237 applications from state schools.[[134]](#cite_note-139) Cambridge, together with Oxford and [Durham](/wiki/Durham_University "Durham University"), was among those universities that adopted formulae in 2009 to rate the [GCSE](/wiki/General_Certificate_of_Secondary_Education "General Certificate of Secondary Education") performance of schools, using data from the [Department for Children, Schools and Families](/wiki/Department_for_Children,_Schools_and_Families "Department for Children, Schools and Families"), and took this into account when assessing university applicants.[[135]](#cite_note-140)

With the release of admissions figures, *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* reported in 2013 that ethnic minority candidates had lower success rates in individual subjects even when they had the same grades as white applicants. The university was criticised for what was seen as institutional discrimination against ethnic minority applicants in favour of white applicants. The university denied the claims of institutional discrimination, stating the figures did not take into account other variables.[[136]](#cite_note-Guardian20130226-141) A subsequent article reported that, in the years 2010 to 2012, ethnic minority applicants to medicine with 3 A\* grades or higher were 20% less likely to gain admission than white applicants with similar grades. The university refused to provide figures for a wider range of subjects, claiming that assembling and releasing such information was excessively costly.[[137]](#cite_note-142)

Given the competitive nature of gaining admission to the University of Cambridge, a number of educational consultancies have emerged to offer support with the application process. Some claim they can improve chances for admission, though these claims have never been independently verified. None of these companies are affiliated with or endorsed by the University of Cambridge. The university informs applicants that all necessary information regarding the application process is publicly available through the university and none of these services is providing any insight not already publicly available to applicants.[[138]](#cite_note-143)

The University of Cambridge has been criticised for admitting a lower percentage of black students, though many apply. Of the 31 colleges at Cambridge, six of them admitted fewer than 10 Black or mixed race students between 2012 and 2016.[[139]](#cite_note-144) Similar criticism exists over a relatively lower admission rate for white working class applicants; in 2019, only 2% of admitted students were white working class.[[140]](#cite_note-145)

In January 2021, Cambridge created foundation courses for disadvantaged students.[[141]](#cite_note-Media-2021-146) While the usual entry requirements are A\*AA in [A-Levels](/wiki/GCE_Advanced_Level "GCE Advanced Level"), the one-year foundation course has 50 places for students who achieve BBB.[[142]](#cite_note-147) If successful on the course, students receive a recognised [CertHE](/wiki/Certificate_of_Higher_Education "Certificate of Higher Education") qualification and can progress to degrees in the arts, humanities, and social sciences at the university.[[141]](#cite_note-Media-2021-146) Candidates include those who have been in care, who are estranged from their families, who have missed significant periods of learning because of health issues, those from low-income backgrounds, and those from schools with few students attending universities.[[141]](#cite_note-Media-2021-146)

### Teaching

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Teaching")]

See also: [Cambridge School (intellectual history)](/wiki/Cambridge_School_(intellectual_history) "Cambridge School (intellectual history)") and [List of professorships at the University of Cambridge](/wiki/List_of_professorships_at_the_University_of_Cambridge "List of professorships at the University of Cambridge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Mathmo_results.jpg/250px-Mathmo_results.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mathmo_results.jpg)

Results for the [Cambridge Mathematical Tripos](/wiki/Mathematical_Tripos "Mathematical Tripos") are read aloud at [Senate House](/wiki/Senate_House,_Cambridge "Senate House, Cambridge") and then tossed from the balcony in accordance with a tradition that began in the 18th century.

The University of Cambridge academic year is divided into three academic terms determined by the statutes of the university.[[143]](#cite_note-148) [Michaelmas term](/wiki/Michaelmas_term "Michaelmas term") lasts from October to December; the [Lent term](/wiki/Lent_term "Lent term") last from January to March; and the [Easter term](/wiki/Easter_term "Easter term") last from April to June.

Within these terms, undergraduate teaching takes place during eight-week periods called full terms. According to university statutes, it is a requirement during these periods that all students live within three miles of the [Church of St Mary the Great](/wiki/Church_of_St_Mary_the_Great,_Cambridge "Church of St Mary the Great, Cambridge"), which is known as keeping term. Students eligible for graduation must fulfil this condition for nine terms (three years) while pursuing a Bachelor of Arts or twelve terms (four years) when pursuing a Master of Science, engineering, or mathematics degree.[[144]](#cite_note-149)

These terms are shorter than those of many other British universities.[[145]](#cite_note-150) Undergraduates are also expected to prepare heavily in the three holidays known as the Christmas, Easter, and Long Vacation holiday periods, which are referred to by the university as vacations rather than holidays; students vacate the premises during these periods but are still expected to be pursuing studies and assignments.

The [Tripos](/wiki/Tripos "Tripos") exam involves a mixture of lectures organised by the university department) and [supervised](/wiki/Supervision_system "Supervision system") and organised by the colleges. Science subjects involve laboratory sessions organised by the departments. The relative importance of these methods of teaching varies according to the needs of the subject. Supervisions are typically weekly hour-long sessions in which small groups of students, usually between one and three students, who meet with a member of the teaching staff or with a doctoral student. Students are normally required to complete an assignment in advance of this supervision, which they then discuss with the supervisor during the session. The assignment is often an essay on a subject assigned by the supervisor, or a problem sheet set by the lecturer. Depending on the subject and college, students sometimes receive between one and four supervisory sessions each week.[[146]](#cite_note-151) This [pedagogical system](/wiki/Tutorial_system "Tutorial system") is often cited as being unique to Oxford, where supervisions are known as [tutorials](/wiki/Tutorial "Tutorial"),[[147]](#cite_note-152) and Cambridge and is sometimes credited with the exceptional nature generally associated with the education at these two world-renowned universities. A tutor named [William Farish](/wiki/William_Farish_(professor) "William Farish (professor)") developed the concept of grading students' work quantitatively at the University of Cambridge in 1792.[[148]](#cite_note-153)

### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Research")]

See also: [Category:Departments of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Category:Departments_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Category:Departments of the University of Cambridge")

The University of Cambridge has research departments and teaching faculties in nearly every academic discipline, with research and lectures conducted by university departments. The colleges are charged with giving or arranging most supervisions, student accommodation, and funding most extracurricular activities. During the 1990s, the University of Cambridge added a substantial number of new specialist research laboratories on several sites around the city, and major expansion continues.[[149]](#cite_note-154) From 2000 to 2006, the University of Cambridge maintained a research partnership with [MIT](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") in the United States, known as the [Cambridge–MIT Institute](/wiki/Cambridge%E2%80%93MIT_Institute "Cambridge–MIT Institute"), which was discontinued after evolving into what is now called the CMI Partnership Programme.

### Graduation tradition and ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Graduation tradition and ceremony")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Cambridge_University_graduation_enter_Senate_House.jpg/250px-Cambridge_University_graduation_enter_Senate_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cambridge_University_graduation_enter_Senate_House.jpg)

Graduands enter [Senate House](/wiki/Senate_House,_Cambridge "Senate House, Cambridge") at a graduation ceremony.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Cmglee_Cambridge_graduation_Leszek_Borysiewicz.jpg/250px-Cmglee_Cambridge_graduation_Leszek_Borysiewicz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cmglee_Cambridge_graduation_Leszek_Borysiewicz.jpg)

Vice-Chancellor Professor Sir [Leszek Borysiewicz](/wiki/Leszek_Borysiewicz "Leszek Borysiewicz") in academic dress, after conferring degrees at the Senate House in July 2014

The university's governing body, [Regent House](/wiki/Regent_House "Regent House"), manages and votes on graduations. A formal meeting of Regent House, known as a [congregation](/wiki/Congregation_(university) "Congregation (university)"), is held for this purpose,[[150]](#cite_note-Ceremony-155) which is typically the final act during which all university procedures for undergraduate and graduate students and other degrees are finalised. After degrees are approved, candidates for graduation are required to request their respective college presents them during commencement congregation.

Graduates receiving an undergraduate degree wear the [academic dress](/wiki/Academic_dress_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Academic dress of the University of Cambridge") to which they are entitled prior to graduating; for example, most students becoming Bachelor of Arts graduates wear undergraduate gowns and not BA gowns. Graduates receiving a post-graduate degree wear the academic dress that they were entitled to before graduating if their first degree was also from the University of Cambridge; if their first degree was from another university, they wear the academic dress of the degree that they are about to receive. The BA gown without the strings is worn if the graduate is 24 years old or younger, and the MA gown without strings is worn if the graduate is 24 years old or older.[[151]](#cite_note-156) Graduates are presented their degrees in [Senate House](/wiki/Senate_House,_Cambridge "Senate House, Cambridge") by each respective college in order of foundation or recognition by the university, except for the university's royal colleges.

During the University of Cambridge's congregation ceremony, graduands are brought forth by the [Praelector](/wiki/Praelector "Praelector") of their respective college, who takes them by the right hand and presents them to the vice-chancellor to receive the degree they have earned. The Praelector presents graduands with the following [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") statement, substituting "\_\_\_\_" with the name of the degree and substituting "woman" for "man" if the graduate is female:

> "*Dignissima domina, Domina Procancellaria et tota Academia praesento vobis hunc virum quem scio tam moribus quam doctrina esse idoneum ad gradum assequendum \_\_\_\_\_; idque tibi fide mea praesto totique Academiae.*

The Latin statement translates in English as, "Most worthy Vice-Chancellor and the whole University, I present to you this man whom I know to be suitable as much by character as by learning to proceed to the degree of \_\_\_\_; for which I pledge my faith to you and to the whole University."

After presentation, the graduate is called by name and kneels before the vice-chancellor and proffers their hands to the vice-chancellor, who clasps them and then confers the degree through the following Latin statement, known as the [Trinitarian formula](/wiki/Trinitarian_formula "Trinitarian formula") (*in nomine Patris*), which may be omitted at the request of the graduand:

"*Auctoritate mihi commissa admitto te ad gradum \_\_\_\_, in nomine Patris et Filii et Spiritus Sancti*, which translates in English as: "By the authority committed to me, I admit you to the degree of \_\_\_\_, in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit."

The new graduate then rises, bows, and leaves the Senate House through the Doctor's door in [Senate House Passage](/wiki/Trinity_Lane "Trinity Lane"), where they receive their degree certificate.[[150]](#cite_note-Ceremony-155)

### Libraries and museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Libraries and museums")]

Main article: [Libraries of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Libraries_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Libraries of the University of Cambridge")

See also: [Category:Museums of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Category:Museums_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Category:Museums of the University of Cambridge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Cmglee_Cambridge_Trinity_College_Neviles_Court.jpg/250px-Cmglee_Cambridge_Trinity_College_Neviles_Court.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cmglee_Cambridge_Trinity_College_Neviles_Court.jpg)

Trinity College's [Wren Library](/wiki/Wren_Library "Wren Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/FitzwilliamMuseum.jpg/250px-FitzwilliamMuseum.jpg)](/wiki/File:FitzwilliamMuseum.jpg)

[Fitzwilliam Museum](/wiki/Fitzwilliam_Museum "Fitzwilliam Museum"), the art and antiquities museum of the University of Cambridge

The University of Cambridge has [116 libraries](/wiki/Libraries_of_Cambridge_University "Libraries of Cambridge University").[[152]](#cite_note-157) [Cambridge University Library](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Library "Cambridge University Library"), which holds more than eight million volumes, is the central research library. It is a [legal deposit](/wiki/Legal_deposit "Legal deposit") library, which entitles it to request a free copy of every book published in the UK and Ireland.[[153]](#cite_note-158)

In addition to the University Library and its dependents, almost every faculty or department has a specialised library; for example, the History Faculty's [Seeley Historical Library](/wiki/Seeley_Historical_Library "Seeley Historical Library") houses in excess of 100,000 books. Every college also maintains a library, partly for the purpose of undergraduate teaching; older colleges often possess many early books and manuscripts in a separate library. For example, [Trinity College's](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Cambridge "Trinity College, Cambridge") [Wren Library](/wiki/Wren_Library,_Cambridge "Wren Library, Cambridge") houses more than 200,000 books printed before 1800 and [Corpus Christi College's](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_College,_Cambridge "Corpus Christi College, Cambridge") [Parker Library](/wiki/Parker_Library,_Corpus_Christi_College "Parker Library, Corpus Christi College") has more than 600 [medieval manuscripts](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript"), representing one of the largest such collections in the world. [Churchill Archives Centre](/wiki/Churchill_Archives_Centre "Churchill Archives Centre") on the campus of [Churchill College](/wiki/Churchill_College "Churchill College") houses the official papers of former British prime ministers [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") and [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher").

The university operates eight arts, cultural, and scientific museums, and a [botanical garden](/wiki/Botanical_garden "Botanical garden").[[154]](#cite_note-159) [Fitzwilliam Museum](/wiki/Fitzwilliam_Museum "Fitzwilliam Museum") is the art and antiquities museum; [Kettle's Yard](/wiki/Kettle%27s_Yard "Kettle's Yard") is the university's [contemporary art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art") gallery; the [Museum of Archaeology and Anthropology](/wiki/Museum_of_Archaeology_and_Anthropology,_University_of_Cambridge "Museum of Archaeology and Anthropology, University of Cambridge") houses the university's collections of local antiquities along with archaeological and [ethnographic](/wiki/Ethnography "Ethnography") artefacts from around the world; [Cambridge University Museum of Zoology](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Museum_of_Zoology "Cambridge University Museum of Zoology") houses a wide range of [zoological](/wiki/Zoology "Zoology") specimens from around the world and is known for its iconic [finback whale](/wiki/Finback_whale "Finback whale") skeleton that hangs outside the museum. Cambridge University Museum of Zoology also holds specimens collected by [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin"), an 1831 University of Cambridge alumnus. Other museums include the [Museum of Classical Archaeology](/wiki/Museum_of_Classical_Archaeology,_Cambridge "Museum of Classical Archaeology, Cambridge"), [Whipple Museum of the History of Science](/wiki/Whipple_Museum_of_the_History_of_Science "Whipple Museum of the History of Science"), [Sedgwick Museum of Earth Sciences](/wiki/Sedgwick_Museum_of_Earth_Sciences "Sedgwick Museum of Earth Sciences"), the university's geology museum, which displays some of Darwin's geological specimens and equipment (Darwin had studied under Adam Sedgwick, and wrote "I a geologist" in a notebook in 1838), and [Polar Museum](/wiki/Scott_Polar_Research_Institute#The_Polar_Museum "Scott Polar Research Institute"), part of the [Scott Polar Research Institute](/wiki/Scott_Polar_Research_Institute "Scott Polar Research Institute"), which is dedicated to [Captain Scott](/wiki/Captain_Scott "Captain Scott") and his men and focuses on the exploration of the [Polar Regions](/wiki/Polar_regions_of_Earth "Polar regions of Earth"). [Cambridge University Botanic Garden](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Botanic_Garden "Cambridge University Botanic Garden"), created in 1831, is the university's botanical garden.

### Publishing and assessments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Publishing and assessments")]

See also: [Cambridge University Press](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Press "Cambridge University Press")

The university's publishing arm, [Cambridge University Press & Assessment](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Press_%26_Assessment "Cambridge University Press & Assessment"), is the oldest printer and publisher in the world and the second largest [university press](/wiki/University_press "University press") in the world.[[155]](#cite_note-160)[[156]](#cite_note-161) It is also the largest department of the university by financial income, reporting income above £800 million.[[157]](#cite_note-162)

The university established its Local Examination Syndicate in 1858, now known as [Cambridge University Press & Assessment](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Press_%26_Assessment "Cambridge University Press & Assessment") after its merger with Cambridge University Press. It is the largest assessment agency in Europe. Cambridge University Press & Assessment plays a leading role in researching, developing, and delivering assessments around the world.[[158]](#cite_note-163)

### Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Awards")]

Main article: [Awards and prizes of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Awards_and_prizes_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Awards and prizes of the University of Cambridge")

The University of Cambridge issues a number of prestigious awards and prizes annually to accomplished University of Cambridge faculty and students. It also issues some awards to those of varying global academic accomplishment regardless of whether their recipient is affiliated with the University of Cambridge. Some of these awards and prizes rank among the world's most estimable academic and intellectual accomplishments. Among the most prominent of them are:

* [Adam Smith Prize](/wiki/Adam_Smith_Prize "Adam Smith Prize"), awarded annually to the university's top-performing student in economics
* [Adams Prize](/wiki/Adams_Prize "Adams Prize"), awarded annually by University of Cambridge mathematics faculty to a UK resident in recognition of distinguished research in mathematics
* [Browne Medal](/wiki/Browne_Medal "Browne Medal"), awarded annually to students who win the [Latin](/wiki/Latin_poetry "Latin poetry") and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_literature "Greek literature") poetry competition
* [Carus Greek Testament Prizes](/wiki/Carus_Greek_Testament_Prizes "Carus Greek Testament Prizes"), a prize issued to winners of an annual competition of the university's undergraduate and graduate in [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") translation of [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") passeges
* [Chancellor's Gold Medal](/wiki/Chancellor%27s_Gold_Medal "Chancellor's Gold Medal"), a prize issued to winners of the university's annual poetry competition
* [Porson Prize](/wiki/Porson_Prize "Porson Prize"), a prize for students who develop the best Greek composition
* [Raymond Horton-Smith Prize](/wiki/Raymond_Horton-Smith_Prize "Raymond Horton-Smith Prize"), awarded annually to the [University of Cambridge Medical School](/wiki/School_of_Clinical_Medicine,_University_of_Cambridge "School of Clinical Medicine, University of Cambridge") student for the best medical school thesis
* [Seatonian Prize](/wiki/Seatonian_Prize "Seatonian Prize"), awarded annually for the best English language poem on a sacred subject
* [Senior Wrangler](/wiki/Senior_Wrangler "Senior Wrangler"), awarded annually to the university's top performing student on the Part II of [Mathematical Tripos](/wiki/Mathematical_Tripos "Mathematical Tripos")[[28]](#cite_note-Forfar_1996-29)
* [Thirlwall Prize](/wiki/Thirlwall_Prize "Thirlwall Prize"), awarded every other year for the best essay about British literature or history
* [Thomas Bond Sprague Prize](/wiki/Thomas_Bond_Sprague_Prize "Thomas Bond Sprague Prize"), awarded annually to the top performing [Part III of the Mathematical Tripos](/wiki/Part_III_of_the_Mathematical_Tripos "Part III of the Mathematical Tripos") student in the areas of probability, statistics, finance and optimisation.
* [Tyson Medal](/wiki/Tyson_Medal "Tyson Medal"), awarded annually to the top [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") student
* [Mayhew Prize](/wiki/Mayhew_Prize "Mayhew Prize"), awarded annually to the top performing [Part III of the Mathematical Tripos](/wiki/Part_III_of_the_Mathematical_Tripos "Part III of the Mathematical Tripos") student in areas of Applied Mathematics and Theoretical Physics (DAMTP)

University of Cambridge's [national league table](/wiki/Rankings_of_universities_in_the_United_Kingdom "Rankings of universities in the United Kingdom") performance over the past ten years

Owing to its age[[165]](#cite_note-BhopalMyers-170)[[166]](#cite_note-171) and its social and academic status,[[167]](#cite_note-Abbott-172)[[168]](#cite_note-Dent-173) the University of Cambridge is considered to be one of Britain's most prestigious or elite universities[[169]](#cite_note-174)[[170]](#cite_note-175)[[171]](#cite_note-176) and to form, along with the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"), a top two that stand above other UK universities in this regard.[[165]](#cite_note-BhopalMyers-170)[[167]](#cite_note-Abbott-172)[[168]](#cite_note-Dent-173)[[172]](#cite_note-177)

As of 2025,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit) the University of Cambridge was ranked sixth in the world by *[QS Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")*,[[163]](#cite_note-QS_World_University_Rankings-168) the *[ARWU](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities")* ranked Cambridge fourth in the world,[[162]](#cite_note-Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities-167) while *[Times Higher Education](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education "Times Higher Education")* ranked Cambridge fifth in the world in its 2025 rankings (released in 2024).[[164]](#cite_note-THE_World_University_Rankings-169)

In 2022, the *QS World University Rankings by subject* ranked a number of Cambridge's programmes among the world's best: Cambridge's arts and humanities programme was ranked second-best in the world; the engineering and technology programme was ranked second-best in the world; the life sciences and medicine programme was ranked fourth-best in the world; the natural sciences program was ranked third-best in the world; and the social sciences and management programme was ranked fourth-best in the world.[[173]](#cite_note-178)

As of 2025,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit) the University of Cambridge has been ranked as the top public university in the world for twelve years straight by the [Center for World University Rankings](/wiki/Center_for_World_University_Rankings "Center for World University Rankings").[[174]](#cite_note-179)

## Student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Student life")]

See also: [Academic dress of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Academic_dress_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Academic dress of the University of Cambridge") and [List of social activities at the University of Cambridge](/wiki/List_of_social_activities_at_the_University_of_Cambridge "List of social activities at the University of Cambridge")

### Formal halls and May balls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Formal halls and May balls")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Clare_Bridge%2C_May_Ball_2005.jpg/250px-Clare_Bridge%2C_May_Ball_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clare_Bridge,_May_Ball_2005.jpg)

The bridge over the [River Cam](/wiki/River_Cam "River Cam") at [Clare College](/wiki/Clare_College,_Cambridge "Clare College, Cambridge") during the 2005 [May Ball in Cambridge](/wiki/May_Ball_in_Cambridge "May Ball in Cambridge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Cmglee_Cambridge_University_Centre_dining_hall.jpg/250px-Cmglee_Cambridge_University_Centre_dining_hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cmglee_Cambridge_University_Centre_dining_hall.jpg)

The University Centre main dining hall

One privilege of student life at the University of Cambridge is the opportunity to attend formal dinners at a student's respective college, known as [Formal Hall](/wiki/Formal_(university) "Formal (university)") that are held regularly during academic terms and daily at some of the university's colleges. During Formal Hall, students typically sit down for a meal in their [gowns](/wiki/Academic_gown "Academic gown") while [fellows](/wiki/Fellow "Fellow") and sometimes guests eat separately at a so-called High Table. The beginning and end of the function is usually marked with [grace](/wiki/Grace_(prayer) "Grace (prayer)"), which is said in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"). Special Formal Halls are organised for Christmas and the Commemoration of Benefactors.[[175]](#cite_note-180)

After the exam period, [May Week](/wiki/May_Week "May Week") is held during which it is customary to celebrate by attending [May Balls](/wiki/May_Ball "May Ball"), which are all-night lavish parties held in the colleges where food, drinks, and entertainment are provided. So-called Suicide Sunday, the first day of May Week, is a popular date for [garden parties](/wiki/Garden_party "Garden party").[[176]](#cite_note-181)

### JCR and MCR

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: JCR and MCR")]

In addition to university-wide representation, students can participate in their own college student unions, which are known as [Junior Combination Room](/wiki/Common_room_(university) "Common room (university)") (JCR) for undergraduates and Middle Combination Room (MCR) for post-graduates. These serve as a link between college staff and members and include officers elected annually between the fellow students; individual JCR and MCRs also report to [Cambridge Students' Union](/wiki/Cambridge_Students%27_Union "Cambridge Students' Union"), which offers training courses for some of the positions within the body.[[177]](#cite_note-182)

### Societies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Societies")]

Main page: [Category:Clubs and societies of the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Category:Clubs_and_societies_of_the_University_of_Cambridge "Category:Clubs and societies of the University of Cambridge")

See also: [Cambridge Union](/wiki/Cambridge_Union "Cambridge Union") and [Cambridge University Moral Sciences Club](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Moral_Sciences_Club "Cambridge University Moral Sciences Club")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Stephen_Fry_at_the_Cambridge_Union.jpg/250px-Stephen_Fry_at_the_Cambridge_Union.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stephen_Fry_at_the_Cambridge_Union.jpg)

[Stephen Fry](/wiki/Stephen_Fry "Stephen Fry") at [Cambridge Union](/wiki/Cambridge_Union "Cambridge Union"), the world's oldest continuing running debate society

Numerous student-run societies exist at the University of Cambridge designed to encourage students who share common passions or interests to periodically meet or discuss these interests. As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit), there were 751 registered societies at the university.[[178]](#cite_note-183) In addition to these, individual colleges often promote their own societies and sports teams.

Although technically independent from the university, [Cambridge Union](/wiki/Cambridge_Union "Cambridge Union"), a globally renowned debate organisation and the oldest debate organisation in the world, offers students high-level debate and public speaking experience. Drama societies include the [Cambridge University Amateur Dramatic Club](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Amateur_Dramatic_Club "Cambridge University Amateur Dramatic Club") (CUADC) and the comedy club [Footlights](/wiki/Footlights "Footlights"), whose alumni include many well-known show business personalities. The university's [Chamber Orchestra](/wiki/Chamber_Orchestra "Chamber Orchestra"), composed entirely of university students, offers a range of orchestra programs, including symphonies.

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main page: [Category:Sport at the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Category:Sport_at_the_University_of_Cambridge "Category:Sport at the University of Cambridge")

See also: [Cambridge University Cricket Club](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Cricket_Club "Cambridge University Cricket Club"), [List of Cambridge University Boat Race crews](/wiki/List_of_Cambridge_University_Boat_Race_crews "List of Cambridge University Boat Race crews"), and [Cambridge University Polo Club](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Polo_Club "Cambridge University Polo Club")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Cambridge_boathouses_-_Goldie.jpg/250px-Cambridge_boathouses_-_Goldie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cambridge_boathouses_-_Goldie.jpg)

The boathouse of the [Cambridge University Boat Club](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Boat_Club "Cambridge University Boat Club")

[Rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") is one of the most popular sports at the University of Cambridge, and there are competitions between colleges, notably the [bumps races](/wiki/Bumps_race "Bumps race"). The University of Cambridge's rowing competition against [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") is known as the [Boat Race](/wiki/Boat_Race "Boat Race"). [Varsity matches](/wiki/List_of_British_and_Irish_varsity_matches "List of British and Irish varsity matches") against Oxford also exist in other sports, including [cricket](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Cricket_Club "Cambridge University Cricket Club"), [rugby](/wiki/Cambridge_University_R.U.F.C. "Cambridge University R.U.F.C."), [chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess"), and [tiddlywinks](/wiki/Tiddlywinks "Tiddlywinks"). Athletes who representing the university in a varsity match are entitled to a [Blue](/wiki/Cambridge_Blue "Cambridge Blue") or a Half Blue, depending on the sport and other criteria.[[179]](#cite_note-Hawks-2-184)

The [University of Cambridge Sports Centre](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge_Sports_Centre "University of Cambridge Sports Centre") opened in August 2013. Phase one included a sports hall, a fitness suite, a strength and conditioning room, a multi-purpose room, and [Eton](/wiki/Eton_Fives "Eton Fives") and [Rugby fives](/wiki/Rugby_fives "Rugby fives") courts. Phase two of its development included five glass-backed [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)") courts and a team training room. Future phases include indoor and outdoor tennis courts and a swimming pool.[[180]](#cite_note-185)

The university also has an [athletic track](/wiki/Cambridge_%26_Coleridge_Athletic_Club "Cambridge & Coleridge Athletic Club") at [Wilberforce Road](/wiki/Wilberforce_Road "Wilberforce Road"), an indoor [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") school, and [Fenner's](/wiki/Fenner%27s "Fenner's"), the cricket ground for [Cambridge University Cricket Club](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Cricket_Club "Cambridge University Cricket Club"). The university has an ice hockey club called [Cambridge University Ice Hockey Club](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Ice_Hockey_Club "Cambridge University Ice Hockey Club"). The [Hawks' Club](/wiki/Hawks%27_Club "Hawks' Club") is a private members' club for the university's leading sportsmen.[[179]](#cite_note-Hawks-2-184) The Ospreys are the equivalent female club.

### Student newspapers and radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Student newspapers and radio")]

See also: [Category:Publications associated with the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Category:Publications_associated_with_the_University_of_Cambridge "Category:Publications associated with the University of Cambridge")

Cambridge's oldest student newspaper is *[Varsity](/wiki/Varsity_(Cambridge) "Varsity (Cambridge)")*. Established in 1947, notable figures who have edited the newspaper include [Jeremy Paxman](/wiki/Jeremy_Paxman "Jeremy Paxman"), [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") media editor [Amol Rajan](/wiki/Amol_Rajan "Amol Rajan"), and *[Vogue](/wiki/Vogue_(magazine) "Vogue (magazine)")* international editor [Suzy Menkes](/wiki/Suzy_Menkes "Suzy Menkes"). The student newspaper also has featured the early writings of [Zadie Smith](/wiki/Zadie_Smith "Zadie Smith"), who appeared in *Varsity*'s literary anthology offshoot *[The Mays](/wiki/The_Mays "The Mays")*, [Robert Webb](/wiki/Robert_Webb "Robert Webb"), [Tristram Hunt](/wiki/Tristram_Hunt "Tristram Hunt"), and [Tony Wilson](/wiki/Tony_Wilson "Tony Wilson").

*Varsity* has a circulation of 9,000 and is the only student publication published weekly. News stories from *Varsity* have appeared in *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*, *[The Sunday Times](/wiki/The_Sunday_Times "The Sunday Times")*, *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*, *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* and *[The i Paper](/wiki/The_i_Paper "The i Paper")*.

Other student publications include *[The Cambridge Student](/wiki/The_Cambridge_Student "The Cambridge Student")*, which is funded by [Cambridge Students' Union](/wiki/Cambridge_Students%27_Union "Cambridge Students' Union") and is published fortnightly, *[The Tab](/wiki/The_Tab "The Tab")*, and *[The Mays](/wiki/The_Mays "The Mays")* Founded by two University of Cambridge students in 2009, *The Tab* is an online media outlet featuring light-hearted features content. *The Mays* is a literary anthology including student prose, poetry, and visual art from both University of Cambridge and [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford_University "Oxford University") students. Founded in 1992 by three Cambridge students, the anthology publishes once a year and is overseen by Varsity Publications Ltd., the same body responsible for *Varsity*. Another literary journal, *Notes*, is published roughly twice per term. Additionally, many colleges have their own student-run publications.

The student radio station, [Cam FM](/wiki/Cam_FM "Cam FM"), is run jointly by University of Cambridge and [Anglia Ruskin University](/wiki/Anglia_Ruskin_University "Anglia Ruskin University") students. The station holds an [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") licence (frequency 97.2 MHz), and hosts a mixture of music, talk, and sports shows.

### Student Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Student Union")]

Main article: [Cambridge Students' Union](/wiki/Cambridge_Students%27_Union "Cambridge Students' Union")

All students at the University of Cambridge are represented by [Cambridge Students' Union](/wiki/Cambridge_Students%27_Union "Cambridge Students' Union"), which was founded in 2020 as a merger of two student unions, [Cambridge University Students' Union](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Students%27_Union "Cambridge University Students' Union") (CUSU) and the Graduate Union (GU). CUSU previously represented all University students, and GU represented graduate students.[[181]](#cite_note-186)[[182]](#cite_note-187)

The eight most important positions in Cambridge Students' Union are occupied by [sabbatical officers](/wiki/Sabbatical_officer "Sabbatical officer").[[183]](#cite_note-188) In 2020, the sabbatical officers were elected with a turnout of 20.88% of the whole student body.[[184]](#cite_note-189)

In 2021, Cambridge Students' Union launched a petition opposing the financial collaboration between the university and the government of [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") that was worth £400m. The Union cited a "values gap" and threat to "academic freedom and institutional autonomy" following the release of internal UAE documents. Citing UAE's history of violating international human rights laws, it warned that university staff were vulnerable under the partnership to repression by gender, sexuality, or freedom of expression.[[185]](#cite_note-190)

In 2023, 72% of the Students' Union voted in favour of hosting talks regarding the removal of all animal products from cafes and canteens operated by the university's catering services. The students backed [vegan](/wiki/Veganism "Veganism") food in response to threats to the climate and biodiversity. The vote is non-binding since the university controls the catering service. The vote was supported by the student chapter of [Plant-Based Universities](/wiki/Vegan_school_meal#Plant-Based_Universities_campaign "Vegan school meal").[[186]](#cite_note-191) After the vote, [Darwin College](/wiki/Darwin_College,_Cambridge "Darwin College, Cambridge") decided to serve only vegan food at its [May Ball](/wiki/May_Ball_in_Cambridge "May Ball in Cambridge") in 2023.[[187]](#cite_note-192)

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Politics")]

A protest in [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge") with an attendance of more than a thousand students and residents – the city's largest demonstration – called on the University of Cambridge to [divest](/wiki/Divestment "Divestment") from Israel over Israel's actions in the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") during the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war").[[188]](#cite_note-varoctlarge-193)[[189]](#cite_note-194) Students and staff also walked out of lectures in protest over the same issues.[[190]](#cite_note-195)

[Students and staff](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Cambridge_people "List of University of Cambridge people") at the University of Cambridge wrote an open letter to the university, with more than 1,400 signatories, demanding it acknowledge the "slaughter of innocent Palestinians", "sever financial ties with Israel" as it had with Russia following the [invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"), and demanding it investigate its financial ties with [arms manufacturers](/wiki/Arms_industry "Arms industry") that potentially supplied to Israel, mentioning, among others, [Plasan](/wiki/Plasan "Plasan") and [Caterpillar](/wiki/Caterpillar_Inc. "Caterpillar Inc.").[[188]](#cite_note-varoctlarge-193)[[191]](#cite_note-varNov-196)

The University of Cambridge has produced many distinguished alumni in various fields. As of 2020,[[192]](#cite_note-197)[[193]](#cite_note-198) 70 alumni have won Nobel Prizes.
