Highly notable University of Cambridge alumni by specialty include:

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [Faculty of Education, University of Cambridge](/wiki/Faculty_of_Education,_University_of_Cambridge "Faculty of Education, University of Cambridge")

Notable alumni in academia include the founders and early professors of [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), including [John Harvard](/wiki/John_Harvard_(clergyman) "John Harvard (clergyman)") himself; [Emily Davies](/wiki/Emily_Davies "Emily Davies"), founder of [Girton College](/wiki/Girton_College,_Cambridge "Girton College, Cambridge") at Cambridge, the first residential higher education institution for women, and [John Haden Badley](/wiki/John_Haden_Badley "John Haden Badley"), founder of the first mixed-sex [public school](/wiki/Public_school_(United_Kingdom) "Public school (United Kingdom)") (i.e. private) in England; [Anil Kumar Gain](/wiki/Anil_Kumar_Gain "Anil Kumar Gain"), 20th century mathematician and founder of the [Vidyasagar University](/wiki/Vidyasagar_University "Vidyasagar University") in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), [Siram Govindarajulu Naidu](/wiki/S._Venkataramadas_Nayudu "S. Venkataramadas Nayudu"), founder and vice chancellor of [Sri Venkateswara University](/wiki/Sri_Venkateswara_University "Sri Venkateswara University"); and [Menachem Ben-Sasson](/wiki/Menachem_Ben-Sasson "Menachem Ben-Sasson"), president of [Hebrew University of Jerusalem](/wiki/Hebrew_University_of_Jerusalem "Hebrew University of Jerusalem").

### Humanities, music, and art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Humanities, music, and art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Thomas_Cranmer_by_Gerlach_Flicke.jpg/250px-Thomas_Cranmer_by_Gerlach_Flicke.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Cranmer_by_Gerlach_Flicke.jpg)

[Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), who led the [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Allama_Iqbal.jpg/250px-Allama_Iqbal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Allama_Iqbal.jpg)

[Muhammad Iqbal](/wiki/Muhammad_Iqbal "Muhammad Iqbal"), philosopher and poet

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Ludwig_Wittgenstein.jpg/250px-Ludwig_Wittgenstein.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ludwig_Wittgenstein.jpg)

[Ludwig Wittgenstein](/wiki/Ludwig_Wittgenstein "Ludwig Wittgenstein"), philosopher

In the humanities, Greek studies were inaugurated at the University of Cambridge in the early sixteenth century by [Desiderius Erasmus](/wiki/Desiderius_Erasmus "Desiderius Erasmus"); contributions to the field were made by [Richard Bentley](/wiki/Richard_Bentley "Richard Bentley") and [Richard Porson](/wiki/Richard_Porson "Richard Porson"). [John Chadwick](/wiki/John_Chadwick "John Chadwick") was associated with [Michael Ventris](/wiki/Michael_Ventris "Michael Ventris") in the decipherment of [Linear B](/wiki/Linear_B "Linear B"). The Latinist [A. E. Housman](/wiki/A._E._Housman "A. E. Housman") taught at the university but is more widely known for his contributions as a poet. [Simon Ockley](/wiki/Simon_Ockley "Simon Ockley") made a significant contribution to [Arabic Studies](/wiki/Arabist "Arabist").

University of Cambridge academics include economists such as [John Maynard Keynes](/wiki/John_Maynard_Keynes "John Maynard Keynes"), [Thomas Malthus](/wiki/Thomas_Malthus "Thomas Malthus"), [Alfred Marshall](/wiki/Alfred_Marshall "Alfred Marshall"), [Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman"), [Joan Robinson](/wiki/Joan_Robinson "Joan Robinson"), [Piero Sraffa](/wiki/Piero_Sraffa "Piero Sraffa"), [Ha-Joon Chang](/wiki/Ha-Joon_Chang "Ha-Joon Chang"), and [Amartya Sen](/wiki/Amartya_Sen "Amartya Sen"). Notable philosophers include [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon "Francis Bacon"), [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell"), [Ludwig Wittgenstein](/wiki/Ludwig_Wittgenstein "Ludwig Wittgenstein"), [Leo Strauss](/wiki/Leo_Strauss "Leo Strauss"), [George Santayana](/wiki/George_Santayana "George Santayana"), [G. E. M. Anscombe](/wiki/G._E._M._Anscombe "G. E. M. Anscombe"), [Karl Popper](/wiki/Karl_Popper "Karl Popper"), [Bernard Williams](/wiki/Bernard_Williams "Bernard Williams"), [Allama Muhammad Iqbal](/wiki/Allama_Muhammad_Iqbal "Allama Muhammad Iqbal"), and [G. E. Moore](/wiki/G._E._Moore "G. E. Moore"). Notable alumni historians include [Thomas Babington Macaulay](/wiki/Thomas_Babington_Macaulay,_1st_Baron_Macaulay "Thomas Babington Macaulay, 1st Baron Macaulay"), [Frederic William Maitland](/wiki/Frederic_William_Maitland "Frederic William Maitland"), [Lord Acton](/wiki/Lord_Acton "Lord Acton"), [Joseph Needham](/wiki/Joseph_Needham "Joseph Needham"), [E. H. Carr](/wiki/E._H._Carr "E. H. Carr"), [Hugh Trevor-Roper](/wiki/Hugh_Trevor-Roper "Hugh Trevor-Roper"), [Rhoda Dorsey](/wiki/Rhoda_Dorsey "Rhoda Dorsey"), [E. P. Thompson](/wiki/E._P._Thompson "E. P. Thompson"), [Eric Hobsbawm](/wiki/Eric_Hobsbawm "Eric Hobsbawm"), [Quentin Skinner](/wiki/Quentin_Skinner "Quentin Skinner"), [Niall Ferguson](/wiki/Niall_Ferguson "Niall Ferguson"), [Arthur M. Schlesinger, Jr.](/wiki/Arthur_M._Schlesinger,_Jr. "Arthur M. Schlesinger, Jr."), and [Karl Schweizer](/wiki/Karl_Schweizer "Karl Schweizer").

Notable alumni in religion include [Rowan Williams](/wiki/Rowan_Williams "Rowan Williams"), the former [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury") and his predecessors; [William Tyndale](/wiki/William_Tyndale "William Tyndale"), the biblical translator; [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), [Hugh Latimer](/wiki/Hugh_Latimer "Hugh Latimer"), and [Nicholas Ridley](/wiki/Nicholas_Ridley_(martyr) "Nicholas Ridley (martyr)"), known as the Oxford martyrs from the place of their execution; [Benjamin Whichcote](/wiki/Benjamin_Whichcote "Benjamin Whichcote") and the [Cambridge Platonists](/wiki/Cambridge_Platonists "Cambridge Platonists"); [William Paley](/wiki/William_Paley "William Paley"), the Christian philosopher known primarily for formulating the [teleological argument](/wiki/Teleological_argument "Teleological argument") for the existence of God; [William Wilberforce](/wiki/William_Wilberforce "William Wilberforce") and [Thomas Clarkson](/wiki/Thomas_Clarkson "Thomas Clarkson"), largely responsible for the [abolition](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abolitionism in the United Kingdom") of the [slave trade](/wiki/History_of_slavery "History of slavery"); Evangelical churchman [Charles Simeon](/wiki/Charles_Simeon "Charles Simeon"); [John William Colenso](/wiki/John_William_Colenso "John William Colenso"), the bishop of Natal who interpreted Scripture and its relations with native peoples that seemed dangerously radical at the time; [John Bainbridge Webster](/wiki/John_Bainbridge_Webster "John Bainbridge Webster") and [David F. Ford](/wiki/David_F._Ford "David F. Ford"), theologians; and six winners of the [Templeton Prize](/wiki/Templeton_Prize "Templeton Prize"), the highest accolade in the world associated with the study of religion.

Notable University of Cambridge alumni in the field of musical composition include [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams"), [Charles Villiers Stanford](/wiki/Charles_Villiers_Stanford "Charles Villiers Stanford"), [William Sterndale Bennett](/wiki/William_Sterndale_Bennett "William Sterndale Bennett"), [Orlando Gibbons](/wiki/Orlando_Gibbons "Orlando Gibbons") and, more recently, [Alexander Goehr](/wiki/Alexander_Goehr "Alexander Goehr"), [Thomas Adès](/wiki/Thomas_Ad%C3%A8s "Thomas Adès"), [John Rutter](/wiki/John_Rutter "John Rutter"), [Julian Anderson](/wiki/Julian_Anderson "Julian Anderson"), [Judith Weir](/wiki/Judith_Weir "Judith Weir"), and [Maury Yeston](/wiki/Maury_Yeston "Maury Yeston"). The university has also produced world-renowned instrumentalists and conductors, including [Colin Davis](/wiki/Colin_Davis "Colin Davis"), [John Eliot Gardiner](/wiki/John_Eliot_Gardiner "John Eliot Gardiner"), [Roger Norrington](/wiki/Roger_Norrington "Roger Norrington"), [Trevor Pinnock](/wiki/Trevor_Pinnock "Trevor Pinnock"), [Andrew Manze](/wiki/Andrew_Manze "Andrew Manze"), [Richard Egarr](/wiki/Richard_Egarr "Richard Egarr"), [Mark Elder](/wiki/Mark_Elder "Mark Elder"), [Richard Hickox](/wiki/Richard_Hickox "Richard Hickox"), [Christopher Hogwood](/wiki/Christopher_Hogwood "Christopher Hogwood"), [Andrew Marriner](/wiki/Andrew_Marriner "Andrew Marriner"), [David Munrow](/wiki/David_Munrow "David Munrow"), [Simon Standage](/wiki/Simon_Standage "Simon Standage"), [Endellion Quartet](/wiki/Endellion_Quartet "Endellion Quartet"), and [Fitzwilliam Quartet](/wiki/Fitzwilliam_Quartet "Fitzwilliam Quartet"). Although the university in music predominantly for its contributions to [choral music](/wiki/Choral_music "Choral music"), university alumni in popular music include members of contemporary bands such as [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead"), [Hot Chip](/wiki/Hot_Chip "Hot Chip"), [Procol Harum](/wiki/Procol_Harum "Procol Harum"), [Clean Bandit](/wiki/Clean_Bandit "Clean Bandit"), [Sports Team](/wiki/Sports_Team "Sports Team") songwriter and entertainer [Jonathan King](/wiki/Jonathan_King "Jonathan King"), [Henry Cow](/wiki/Henry_Cow "Henry Cow"), and the singer-songwriter [Nick Drake](/wiki/Nick_Drake "Nick Drake").

Artists [Quentin Blake](/wiki/Quentin_Blake "Quentin Blake"), [Roger Fry](/wiki/Roger_Fry "Roger Fry"), [Rose Ferraby](/wiki/Rose_Ferraby "Rose Ferraby"), and [Julian Trevelyan](/wiki/Julian_Trevelyan "Julian Trevelyan"), sculptors [Antony Gormley](/wiki/Antony_Gormley "Antony Gormley"), [Marc Quinn](/wiki/Marc_Quinn "Marc Quinn"), and [Anthony Caro](/wiki/Anthony_Caro "Anthony Caro"), and photographers [Antony Armstrong-Jones](/wiki/Antony_Armstrong-Jones "Antony Armstrong-Jones"), [Cecil Beaton](/wiki/Cecil_Beaton "Cecil Beaton"), and [Mick Rock](/wiki/Mick_Rock "Mick Rock") are each University of Cambridge alumni.

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Byron_1813_by_Phillips.jpg/250px-Byron_1813_by_Phillips.jpg)](/wiki/File:Byron_1813_by_Phillips.jpg)

[Lord Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron"), English poet

Writers to have studied at the university include the Elizabethan dramatist [Christopher Marlowe](/wiki/Christopher_Marlowe "Christopher Marlowe"), his fellow [University Wits](/wiki/University_Wits "University Wits"), [Thomas Nashe](/wiki/Thomas_Nashe "Thomas Nashe"), and [Robert Greene](/wiki/Robert_Greene_(dramatist) "Robert Greene (dramatist)"), arguably the first professional authors in England, and [John Fletcher](/wiki/John_Fletcher_(playwright) "John Fletcher (playwright)") who collaborated with [Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") on *[The Two Noble Kinsmen](/wiki/The_Two_Noble_Kinsmen "The Two Noble Kinsmen")*, *[Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_(play) "Henry VIII (play)")*, and the lost *[Cardenio](/wiki/Cardenio "Cardenio")* and succeeded him as house playwright for [The King's Men](/wiki/King%27s_Men_(playing_company) "King's Men (playing company)"). [Samuel Pepys](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys "Samuel Pepys") matriculated in 1650, known for his [diary](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys#The_diary "Samuel Pepys"), the original manuscripts of which are now housed in the [Pepys Library](/wiki/Pepys_Library "Pepys Library") at Magdalene College. [Lawrence Sterne](/wiki/Lawrence_Sterne "Lawrence Sterne"), whose novel *[Tristram Shandy](/wiki/Tristram_Shandy "Tristram Shandy")* is judged to have inspired many modern narrative devices and styles. In the following century, the novelists [W. M. Thackeray](/wiki/W._M._Thackeray "W. M. Thackeray"), author of *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(novel) "Vanity Fair (novel)")*, [Charles Kingsley](/wiki/Charles_Kingsley "Charles Kingsley"), author of *[Westward Ho!](/wiki/Westward_Ho! "Westward Ho!")* and *[Water Babies](/wiki/The_Water-Babies,_A_Fairy_Tale_for_a_Land_Baby "The Water-Babies, A Fairy Tale for a Land Baby")*, and [Samuel Butler](/wiki/Samuel_Butler_(novelist) "Samuel Butler (novelist)"), remembered for *[The Way of All Flesh](/wiki/The_Way_of_All_Flesh "The Way of All Flesh")* and *[Erewhon](/wiki/Erewhon "Erewhon")*, are all University of Cambridge alumni.

Ghost story writer [M. R. James](/wiki/M._R._James "M. R. James") served as provost of King's College from 1905 to 1918. Novelist [Amy Levy](/wiki/Amy_Levy "Amy Levy") was the second Jewish woman to attend the university. Modernist writers to have attended the university include [E. M. Forster](/wiki/E._M._Forster "E. M. Forster"), [Rosamond Lehmann](/wiki/Rosamond_Lehmann "Rosamond Lehmann"), [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov"), [Christopher Isherwood](/wiki/Christopher_Isherwood "Christopher Isherwood"), and [Malcolm Lowry](/wiki/Malcolm_Lowry "Malcolm Lowry"). Playwright [J. B. Priestley](/wiki/J._B._Priestley "J. B. Priestley"), physicist and novelist [C. P. Snow](/wiki/C._P._Snow "C. P. Snow"), and children's writer [A. A. Milne](/wiki/A._A._Milne "A. A. Milne") are each early 20th century alumni of the university. They were followed by postmodernists [Patrick White](/wiki/Patrick_White "Patrick White"), [J. G. Ballard](/wiki/J._G._Ballard "J. G. Ballard"), and early postcolonial writer [E. R. Braithwaite](/wiki/E._R._Braithwaite "E. R. Braithwaite"). More recently, alumni include comedy writers [Douglas Adams](/wiki/Douglas_Adams "Douglas Adams"), [Tom Sharpe](/wiki/Tom_Sharpe "Tom Sharpe") and [Howard Jacobson](/wiki/Howard_Jacobson "Howard Jacobson"), the popular novelists [A. S. Byatt](/wiki/A._S._Byatt "A. S. Byatt"), [Salman Rushdie](/wiki/Salman_Rushdie "Salman Rushdie"), [Nick Hornby](/wiki/Nick_Hornby "Nick Hornby"), [Zadie Smith](/wiki/Zadie_Smith "Zadie Smith"), [Louise Dean](/wiki/Louise_Dean_(author) "Louise Dean (author)"), [Robert Harris](/wiki/Robert_Harris_(novelist) "Robert Harris (novelist)"), and [Sebastian Faulks](/wiki/Sebastian_Faulks "Sebastian Faulks"), action writers [Michael Crichton](/wiki/Michael_Crichton "Michael Crichton"), [David Gibbins](/wiki/David_Gibbins "David Gibbins"), and [Jin Yong](/wiki/Jin_Yong "Jin Yong"), and contemporary playwrights and screenwriters, including [Julian Fellowes](/wiki/Julian_Fellowes "Julian Fellowes"), [Stephen Poliakoff](/wiki/Stephen_Poliakoff "Stephen Poliakoff"), [Michael Frayn](/wiki/Michael_Frayn "Michael Frayn"), and [Peter Shaffer](/wiki/Peter_Shaffer "Peter Shaffer"), as well as musical theatre writers [Toby Marlow](/wiki/Toby_Marlow "Toby Marlow") and [Lucy Moss](/wiki/Lucy_Moss "Lucy Moss").

Within poetry, University of Cambridge alumni include the poets [Edmund Spenser](/wiki/Edmund_Spenser "Edmund Spenser"), author of *[The Faerie Queene](/wiki/The_Faerie_Queene "The Faerie Queene")*, metaphysical poets [John Donne](/wiki/John_Donne "John Donne"), who wrote *[For Whom the Bell Tolls](/wiki/For_Whom_the_Bell_Tolls_(disambiguation) "For Whom the Bell Tolls (disambiguation)")*, [George Herbert](/wiki/George_Herbert "George Herbert") and [Andrew Marvell](/wiki/Andrew_Marvell "Andrew Marvell"), and [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton"), who is renowned for *[Paradise Lost](/wiki/Paradise_Lost "Paradise Lost")*, Restoration poet and playwright [John Dryden](/wiki/John_Dryden "John Dryden"), pre-romantic poet [Thomas Gray](/wiki/Thomas_Gray "Thomas Gray") best known his *[Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard](/wiki/Elegy_Written_in_a_Country_Churchyard "Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard")*, [William Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth"), and [Samuel Taylor Coleridge](/wiki/Samuel_Taylor_Coleridge "Samuel Taylor Coleridge"), whose joint work *[Lyrical Ballads](/wiki/Lyrical_Ballads "Lyrical Ballads")* is often cited as marking the beginning of the [Romantic movement](/wiki/Romantic_poetry "Romantic poetry"), later Romantics including [Lord Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron") and the post-romantic [Lord Tennyson](/wiki/Alfred,_Lord_Tennyson "Alfred, Lord Tennyson"), authors of the best known [carpe diem](/wiki/Carpe_diem "Carpe diem") poems, including [Robert Herrick](/wiki/Robert_Herrick_(poet) "Robert Herrick (poet)") known for "[To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time](/wiki/To_the_Virgins,_to_Make_Much_of_Time "To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time")" with the first line "Gather ye rosebuds while ye may", and [Andrew Marvell](/wiki/Andrew_Marvell "Andrew Marvell"), who authored "[To His Coy Mistress](/wiki/To_His_Coy_Mistress "To His Coy Mistress")", classical scholar and lyric poet [A. E. Housman](/wiki/A._E._Housman "A. E. Housman"), war poets [Siegfried Sassoon](/wiki/Siegfried_Sassoon "Siegfried Sassoon") and [Rupert Brooke](/wiki/Rupert_Brooke "Rupert Brooke"), modernist [T. E. Hulme](/wiki/T._E._Hulme "T. E. Hulme"), confessional poets [Ted Hughes](/wiki/Ted_Hughes "Ted Hughes"), [Sylvia Plath](/wiki/Sylvia_Plath "Sylvia Plath"), and [John Berryman](/wiki/John_Berryman "John Berryman"), and, more recently, [Cecil Day-Lewis](/wiki/Cecil_Day-Lewis "Cecil Day-Lewis"), [Joseph Brodsky](/wiki/Joseph_Brodsky "Joseph Brodsky"), [Kathleen Raine](/wiki/Kathleen_Raine "Kathleen Raine"), and [Geoffrey Hill](/wiki/Geoffrey_Hill "Geoffrey Hill").

At least nine [Poets Laureate](/wiki/Poet_Laureate_of_the_United_Kingdom "Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom") graduated from the University of Cambridge. University alumni have also made notable contributions to literary criticism, having produced, among others, [F. R. Leavis](/wiki/F._R._Leavis "F. R. Leavis"), [I. A. Richards](/wiki/I._A._Richards "I. A. Richards"), [C. K. Ogden](/wiki/C._K._Ogden "C. K. Ogden"), and [William Empson](/wiki/William_Empson "William Empson"), often collectively known as the [Cambridge Critics](/wiki/Cambridge_Critics "Cambridge Critics"), the Marxists [Raymond Williams](/wiki/Raymond_Williams "Raymond Williams"), sometimes regarded as the founding father of [cultural studies](/wiki/Cultural_studies "Cultural studies"), and [Terry Eagleton](/wiki/Terry_Eagleton "Terry Eagleton"), author of *Literary Theory: An Introduction*, the most successful academic book ever published, the aesthetician [Harold Bloom](/wiki/Harold_Bloom "Harold Bloom"), new historicist [Stephen Greenblatt](/wiki/Stephen_Greenblatt "Stephen Greenblatt"), and biographical writers including [Lytton Strachey](/wiki/Lytton_Strachey "Lytton Strachey"), a central figure in the [Bloomsbury Group](/wiki/Bloomsbury_Group "Bloomsbury Group"), [Peter Ackroyd](/wiki/Peter_Ackroyd "Peter Ackroyd"), and [Claire Tomalin](/wiki/Claire_Tomalin "Claire Tomalin").

Actors and directors who attended the University of Cambridge include [Ian McKellen](/wiki/Ian_McKellen "Ian McKellen"), [Eleanor Bron](/wiki/Eleanor_Bron "Eleanor Bron"), [Miriam Margolyes](/wiki/Miriam_Margolyes "Miriam Margolyes"), [Derek Jacobi](/wiki/Derek_Jacobi "Derek Jacobi"), [Michael Redgrave](/wiki/Michael_Redgrave "Michael Redgrave"), [James Mason](/wiki/James_Mason "James Mason"), [Emma Thompson](/wiki/Emma_Thompson "Emma Thompson"), [Stephen Fry](/wiki/Stephen_Fry "Stephen Fry"), [Hugh Laurie](/wiki/Hugh_Laurie "Hugh Laurie"), [John Cleese](/wiki/John_Cleese "John Cleese"), [John Oliver](/wiki/John_Oliver "John Oliver"), [Freddie Highmore](/wiki/Freddie_Highmore "Freddie Highmore"), [Eric Idle](/wiki/Eric_Idle "Eric Idle"), [Graham Chapman](/wiki/Graham_Chapman "Graham Chapman"), [Graeme Garden](/wiki/Graeme_Garden "Graeme Garden"), [Tim Brooke-Taylor](/wiki/Tim_Brooke-Taylor "Tim Brooke-Taylor"), [Bill Oddie](/wiki/Bill_Oddie "Bill Oddie"), [Simon Russell Beale](/wiki/Simon_Russell_Beale "Simon Russell Beale"), [Tilda Swinton](/wiki/Tilda_Swinton "Tilda Swinton"), [Thandie Newton](/wiki/Thandie_Newton "Thandie Newton"), [Georgie Henley](/wiki/Georgie_Henley "Georgie Henley"), [Rachel Weisz](/wiki/Rachel_Weisz "Rachel Weisz"), [Sacha Baron Cohen](/wiki/Sacha_Baron_Cohen "Sacha Baron Cohen"), [Tom Hiddleston](/wiki/Tom_Hiddleston "Tom Hiddleston"), [Sara Mohr-Pietsch](/wiki/Sara_Mohr-Pietsch "Sara Mohr-Pietsch"), [Eddie Redmayne](/wiki/Eddie_Redmayne "Eddie Redmayne"), [Dan Stevens](/wiki/Dan_Stevens "Dan Stevens"), [Jamie Bamber](/wiki/Jamie_Bamber "Jamie Bamber"), [Lily Cole](/wiki/Lily_Cole "Lily Cole"), [David Mitchell](/wiki/David_Mitchell_(comedian) "David Mitchell (comedian)"), [Robert Webb](/wiki/Robert_Webb "Robert Webb"), [Richard Ayoade](/wiki/Richard_Ayoade "Richard Ayoade"), [Mel Giedroyc](/wiki/Mel_Giedroyc "Mel Giedroyc"), and [Sue Perkins](/wiki/Sue_Perkins "Sue Perkins"). Directors [Mike Newell](/wiki/Mike_Newell_(director) "Mike Newell (director)"), [Robert Icke](/wiki/Robert_Icke "Robert Icke"), [Sam Mendes](/wiki/Sam_Mendes "Sam Mendes"), [Simon McBurney](/wiki/Simon_McBurney "Simon McBurney"), [Peter Hall](/wiki/Peter_Hall_(director) "Peter Hall (director)"), [Trevor Nunn](/wiki/Trevor_Nunn "Trevor Nunn"), [Stephen Frears](/wiki/Stephen_Frears "Stephen Frears"), [Paul Greengrass](/wiki/Paul_Greengrass "Paul Greengrass"), [Chris Weitz](/wiki/Chris_Weitz "Chris Weitz"), and [John Madden](/wiki/John_Madden_(director) "John Madden (director)") each are alumni of the university.

### Mathematics and sciences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Mathematics and sciences")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Charles_Darwin_1877_%28cropped%29.png/250px-Charles_Darwin_1877_%28cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:Charles_Darwin_1877_(cropped).png)

[Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin"), evolutionary biologist

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/eb/Stephen_Hawking.StarChild.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stephen_Hawking.StarChild.jpg)

[Stephen Hawking](/wiki/Stephen_Hawking "Stephen Hawking"), theoretical physicist and cosmologist

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Isaac_Newton%2C_English_School%2C_1715-20.jpg/250px-Isaac_Newton%2C_English_School%2C_1715-20.jpg)](/wiki/File:Isaac_Newton,_English_School,_1715-20.jpg)

[Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton"), mathematician and physicist who developed [classical mechanics](/wiki/Classical_mechanics "Classical mechanics") and [calculus](/wiki/Calculus "Calculus")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Abdus_Salam_1987.jpg/250px-Abdus_Salam_1987.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abdus_Salam_1987.jpg)

[Abdus Salam](/wiki/Abdus_Salam "Abdus Salam"), a theoretical physicist

[Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton"), who conducted many of his experiments on the grounds of Trinity College, ranks among the most famed University of Cambridge alumni. Other alumni of the university include [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon "Francis Bacon"), who developed the [scientific method](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method") of inquiry, mathematicians [John Dee](/wiki/John_Dee "John Dee") and [Brook Taylor](/wiki/Brook_Taylor "Brook Taylor"), [pure mathematicians](/wiki/Pure_mathematics "Pure mathematics") [G. H. Hardy](/wiki/G._H._Hardy "G. H. Hardy"), [John Edensor Littlewood](/wiki/John_Edensor_Littlewood "John Edensor Littlewood"), [Mary Cartwright](/wiki/Mary_Cartwright "Mary Cartwright"), and [Augustus De Morgan](/wiki/Augustus_De_Morgan "Augustus De Morgan"); [Michael Atiyah](/wiki/Michael_Atiyah "Michael Atiyah"), a geometry specialist; [William Oughtred](/wiki/William_Oughtred "William Oughtred"), inventor of the [logarithmic scale](/wiki/Logarithmic_scale "Logarithmic scale"); [John Wallis](/wiki/John_Wallis "John Wallis"), first to explain the law of acceleration; [Srinivasa Ramanujan](/wiki/Srinivasa_Ramanujan "Srinivasa Ramanujan"), a genius who made substantial contributions to [mathematical analysis](/wiki/Mathematical_analysis "Mathematical analysis"), [number theory](/wiki/Number_theory "Number theory"), [infinite series](/wiki/Infinite_series "Infinite series"), and [continued fractions](/wiki/Continued_fractions "Continued fractions"); and [James Clerk Maxwell](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell "James Clerk Maxwell"), who brought about the second great unification of physics (the first being accredited to Newton) with his classical theory of [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation"). In 1890, mathematician [Philippa Fawcett](/wiki/Philippa_Fawcett "Philippa Fawcett"), a University of Cambridge student, registered the highest score in the Cambridge Mathematical Tripos exams but as a woman was then ineligible to claim the title [Senior Wrangler](/wiki/Senior_Wrangler "Senior Wrangler").

In biology, University of Cambridge alumni include [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin"), famous for developing the theory of [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection") and explaining evolution, is an alumnus of [Christ's College](/wiki/Christ%27s_College,_Cambridge "Christ's College, Cambridge"). Biologists [Francis Crick](/wiki/Francis_Crick "Francis Crick") and [James Watson](/wiki/James_D._Watson "James D. Watson") developed the model explaining the three-dimensional structure of [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") while working at the [Cavendish Laboratory](/wiki/Cavendish_Laboratory "Cavendish Laboratory"); University of Cambridge graduates [Maurice Wilkins](/wiki/Maurice_Wilkins "Maurice Wilkins") and especially [Rosalind Franklin](/wiki/Rosalind_Franklin "Rosalind Franklin") produced key [X-ray crystallography](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography") data, which was shared with Watson by Wilkins. Wilkins went on to verify the proposed structure and win the Nobel Prize with Watson and Crick. More recently, [Ian Wilmut](/wiki/Ian_Wilmut "Ian Wilmut") was part of the team responsible for the first cloning of a mammal ([Dolly the Sheep](/wiki/Dolly_the_Sheep "Dolly the Sheep") in 1996), naturalist and broadcaster [David Attenborough](/wiki/David_Attenborough "David Attenborough"), ethologist [Jane Goodall](/wiki/Jane_Goodall "Jane Goodall"), expert on chimpanzees was a PhD student at the university, anthropologist [Dame Alison Richard](/wiki/Alison_Richard "Alison Richard"), former vice-chancellor of the university, and [Frederick Sanger](/wiki/Frederick_Sanger "Frederick Sanger"), a biochemist known for developing [Sanger sequencing](/wiki/Sanger_sequencing "Sanger sequencing") and receiving two Nobel prizes.

Despite the university's delay in admitting women to its full degree programs, women associated with the University of Cambridge have been at the heart of scientific research throughout the 20th century. Notable female scientists include biochemist [Marjory Stephenson](/wiki/Marjory_Stephenson "Marjory Stephenson"), plant physiologist [Gabrielle Howard](/wiki/Gabrielle_Howard "Gabrielle Howard"), social anthropologist [Audrey Richards](/wiki/Audrey_Richards "Audrey Richards"), psychoanalyst [Alix Strachey](/wiki/Alix_Strachey "Alix Strachey"), who with her husband translated the works of [Sigmund Freud](/wiki/Sigmund_Freud "Sigmund Freud"), [Kavli Prize](/wiki/Kavli_Prize "Kavli Prize")-winner [Brenda Milner](/wiki/Brenda_Milner "Brenda Milner"), responsible for co-discovering specialised brain networks for memory and cognition. Veterinary epidemiologist [Sarah Cleaveland](/wiki/Sarah_Cleaveland "Sarah Cleaveland") has contributed to advances in eliminating [rabies](/wiki/Rabies "Rabies") in the [Serengeti](/wiki/Serengeti "Serengeti").[[194]](#cite_note-199)

The university is widely considered the birthplace of the computer; mathematician and father of the computer [Charles Babbage](/wiki/Charles_Babbage "Charles Babbage") designed the world's first computing system as early as the mid-1800s. Cambridge alumnus [Alan Turing](/wiki/Alan_Turing "Alan Turing") devised the basis for modern computing, and [Maurice Wilkes](/wiki/Maurice_Wilkes "Maurice Wilkes") later created the first programmable computer. The [webcam](/wiki/Webcam "Webcam") was also invented at the University of Cambridge, showing the [Trojan Room coffee pot](/wiki/Trojan_Room_coffee_pot "Trojan Room coffee pot") in the university's computer laboratories.

In physics, [Ernest Rutherford](/wiki/Ernest_Rutherford "Ernest Rutherford"), regarded as the father of [nuclear physics](/wiki/Nuclear_physics "Nuclear physics"), spent much of his life at the university, where he worked closely with [E. J. Williams](/wiki/Evan_James_Williams "Evan James Williams") and [Niels Bohr](/wiki/Niels_Bohr "Niels Bohr"), a major contributor to the understanding of the [atom](/wiki/Atom "Atom"), [J. J. Thomson](/wiki/J._J._Thomson "J. J. Thomson"), discoverer of the [electron](/wiki/Electron "Electron"), [James Chadwick](/wiki/James_Chadwick "James Chadwick"), discoverer of the [neutron](/wiki/Neutron "Neutron"), and [John Cockcroft](/wiki/John_Cockcroft "John Cockcroft") and [Ernest Walton](/wiki/Ernest_Walton "Ernest Walton"), responsible for first splitting the atom. [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer"), leader of the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project") that developed the [atomic bomb](/wiki/Atomic_bomb "Atomic bomb"), also studied under Rutherford and Thomson. [Joan Curran](/wiki/Joan_Curran "Joan Curran") devised the [chaff](/wiki/Chaff_(countermeasure) "Chaff (countermeasure)") technique used during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") to disrupt [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") on [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers")' planes.

University of Cambridge alumni in astronomy include [John Herschel](/wiki/John_Herschel "John Herschel"), [Arthur Eddington](/wiki/Arthur_Eddington "Arthur Eddington"), and [Paul Dirac](/wiki/Paul_Dirac "Paul Dirac"), discoverer of [antimatter](/wiki/Antimatter "Antimatter") and one of the pioneers of [quantum mechanics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics"); [Stephen Hawking](/wiki/Stephen_Hawking "Stephen Hawking"), theoretical physicist and the university's long-serving [Lucasian Professor of Mathematics](/wiki/Lucasian_Professor_of_Mathematics "Lucasian Professor of Mathematics") until 2009; and [Martin Rees](/wiki/Martin_Rees,_Baron_Rees_of_Ludlow "Martin Rees, Baron Rees of Ludlow"), the current [Astronomer Royal](/wiki/Astronomer_Royal "Astronomer Royal") and former Master of Trinity College. [John Polkinghorne](/wiki/John_Polkinghorne "John Polkinghorne"), a mathematician before his ordination to the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") [ministry](/wiki/Minister_(Christianity) "Minister (Christianity)"), received the [Templeton Prize](/wiki/Templeton_Prize "Templeton Prize") for his work reconciling science and religion.

Other significant university alumni in science include [Henry Cavendish](/wiki/Henry_Cavendish "Henry Cavendish"), who discovered [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen"); [Frank Whittle](/wiki/Frank_Whittle "Frank Whittle"), co-inventor of the jet engine; [William Thomson (Lord Kelvin)](/wiki/William_Thomson,_1st_Baron_Kelvin "William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin"), who formulated the original [Laws of Thermodynamics](/wiki/Laws_of_Thermodynamics "Laws of Thermodynamics"); [William Fox Talbot](/wiki/William_Fox_Talbot "William Fox Talbot"), who invented the camera, [Alfred North Whitehead](/wiki/Alfred_North_Whitehead "Alfred North Whitehead"), Einstein's major opponent; [Jagadish Chandra Bose](/wiki/Jagadish_Chandra_Bose "Jagadish Chandra Bose"), one of the fathers of radio science; [Lord Rayleigh](/wiki/John_William_Strutt,_3rd_Baron_Rayleigh "John William Strutt, 3rd Baron Rayleigh"), who made extensive contributions to both theoretical and experimental physics in the 20th century; and [Georges Lemaître](/wiki/Georges_Lema%C3%AEtre "Georges Lemaître"), who first proposed the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") theory.

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Politics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Oliver_Cromwell_by_Samuel_Cooper.jpg/250px-Oliver_Cromwell_by_Samuel_Cooper.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oliver_Cromwell_by_Samuel_Cooper.jpg)

[Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), statesman, politician, and soldier who served as [Lord Protector of England](/wiki/Lord_Protector "Lord Protector")

The University of Cambridge has a strong reputation in the field of politics, having educated:[[195]](#cite_note-200)

* [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), [Lord Protector of England](/wiki/Lord_Protector_of_England "Lord Protector of England") (1653–58)[[196]](#cite_note-201)
* 14 [British Prime Ministers](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom"), including [Robert Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole "Robert Walpole"), who is widely regarded as the first British Prime Minister. The last Cambridge graduate to have served as British Prime Minister was [Stanley Baldwin](/wiki/Stanley_Baldwin "Stanley Baldwin").
* At least 30 foreign heads of state or government, including presidents of India, Ireland, Trinidad and Tobago, Uganda, South Korea, Singapore, and Zambia; along with prime ministers of Australia, Bahrain, Burma, France, India, Jordan, Luxembourg, Malaysia, Malta, Thailand, Pakistan, Poland, Singapore, South Africa, Sri Lanka, and New Zealand
* At least nine monarchs, including Kings [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII"), [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI"), and (current King) [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") of the United Kingdom, King [Peter II of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Peter_II_of_Yugoslavia "Peter II of Yugoslavia"), Queen [Margrethe II of Denmark](/wiki/Margrethe_II_of_Denmark "Margrethe II of Denmark"), and [Queen Sofía of Spain](/wiki/Queen_Sof%C3%ADa_of_Spain "Queen Sofía of Spain"). The university had also educated a large number of royals, including [Prince Edward, Earl of Wessex](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Earl_of_Wessex "Prince Edward, Earl of Wessex")
* Three signatories of the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), [Thomas Lynch Jr.](/wiki/Thomas_Lynch_Jr. "Thomas Lynch Jr."), [Arthur Middleton](/wiki/Arthur_Middleton "Arthur Middleton"), and [Thomas Nelson Jr.](/wiki/Thomas_Nelson_Jr. "Thomas Nelson Jr.")[[197]](#cite_note-202)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Sports")]

By 2016, athletes who are university graduates or attendees had won 194 Olympic medals, including 88 gold medals.[[16]](#cite_note-Hawks-17) Alumni of the university include Chinese six-time world table tennis champion [Deng Yaping](/wiki/Deng_Yaping "Deng Yaping"); sprinter and athletics hero [Harold Abrahams](/wiki/Harold_Abrahams "Harold Abrahams"); inventors of the modern game of football, [H. de Winton](/wiki/H._de_Winton "H. de Winton") and [J. C. Thring](/wiki/J._C._Thring "J. C. Thring"); Indian cricketer Colonel H. H. Shri Sir [Ranjitsinhji Vibhaji II](/wiki/Ranjitsinhji "Ranjitsinhji"); and [George Mallory](/wiki/George_Mallory "George Mallory"), the mountaineer.

### Technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Technology")]

University of Cambridge alumni are responsible some of the world's greatest advances in technology, and several have gone on to found or co-found leading technology companies, including [Demis Hassabis](/wiki/Demis_Hassabis "Demis Hassabis"), the co-founder and chief executive officer of [DeepMind](/wiki/DeepMind "DeepMind"), an [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") subsidiary company of [Alphabet Inc](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc "Alphabet Inc") responsible for [AlphaGo](/wiki/AlphaGo "AlphaGo") and [Sophie Wilson](/wiki/Sophie_Wilson "Sophie Wilson") and [Steve Furber](/wiki/Steve_Furber "Steve Furber"), co-founders of [ARM](/wiki/Arm_Ltd. "Arm Ltd."), a British semiconductor and software design company based in Cambridge.

### Notable current academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Notable current academics")]

The academic staff at the University of Cambridge includes several notable scholars, including [Sriya Iyer](/wiki/Sriya_Iyer "Sriya Iyer"), known for her research in the economics of religion and development;[[198]](#cite_note-203)[[199]](#cite_note-204)[[200]](#cite_note-205) [Graham Virgo](/wiki/Graham_Virgo "Graham Virgo"), specialising in equity and the law of restitution;[[201]](#cite_note-206)[[202]](#cite_note-207)[[203]](#cite_note-208) [Aaron Koller](/wiki/Aaron_Koller "Aaron Koller"), a specialist in [Semitic languages](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages") and Jewish intellectual history;[[204]](#cite_note-209)[[205]](#cite_note-210) [Mihaela van der Schaar](/wiki/Mihaela_van_der_Schaar "Mihaela van der Schaar"), known for her research in AI-enabled personalised medicine.[[206]](#cite_note-211)

## In literature and popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Cambridge&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: In literature and popular culture")]

See also: [List of fictional Cambridge colleges](/wiki/List_of_fictional_Cambridge_colleges "List of fictional Cambridge colleges"); [A Disappearing Number](/wiki/A_Disappearing_Number "A Disappearing Number"); [Cambridge Spies](/wiki/Cambridge_Spies "Cambridge Spies"); [Chariots of Fire](/wiki/Chariots_of_Fire "Chariots of Fire"); [Chariots of Fire (play)](/wiki/Chariots_of_Fire_(play) "Chariots of Fire (play)"); [High Table, Lower Orders](/wiki/High_Table,_Lower_Orders "High Table, Lower Orders"); [Category:Novels set in the University of Cambridge](/wiki/Category:Novels_set_in_the_University_of_Cambridge "Category:Novels set in the University of Cambridge"); [Porterhouse Blue (TV series)](/wiki/Porterhouse_Blue_(TV_series) "Porterhouse Blue (TV series)"); [Psmith](/wiki/Psmith "Psmith"); [Rock 'n' Roll (play)](/wiki/Rock_%27n%27_Roll_(play) "Rock 'n' Roll (play)"); [The Reeve's Tale](/wiki/The_Reeve%27s_Tale "The Reeve's Tale"); and [The Riddle of the Sphinx (Inside No. 9)](/wiki/The_Riddle_of_the_Sphinx_(Inside_No._9) "The Riddle of the Sphinx (Inside No. 9)")

Throughout its history, the University of Cambridge has frequently been featured in literature, artistic works, television, and film. Cambridge was mentioned as early as the 14th century in [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer")'s *[The Canterbury Tales](/wiki/The_Canterbury_Tales "The Canterbury Tales")*. In *[The Reeve's Tale](/wiki/The_Reeve%27s_Tale "The Reeve's Tale")*, the two main fictional characters are students at a University of Cambridge college called Soler Halle, which is believed to refer to [King's Hall](/wiki/King%27s_Hall,_Cambridge "King's Hall, Cambridge") and is now part of [Trinity College](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Cambridge "Trinity College, Cambridge").[[207]](#cite_note-212) The university has been the setting for all or parts of numerous novels, including [Douglas Adams](/wiki/Douglas_Adams "Douglas Adams")' *[Dirk Gently's Holistic Detective Agency](/wiki/Dirk_Gently%27s_Holistic_Detective_Agency "Dirk Gently's Holistic Detective Agency")*, [Rose Macaulay](/wiki/Rose_Macaulay "Rose Macaulay")'s *[They Were Defeated](/wiki/They_Were_Defeated "They Were Defeated")*,[[208]](#cite_note-213) and [Tom Sharpe](/wiki/Tom_Sharpe "Tom Sharpe")'s *[Porterhouse Blue](/wiki/Porterhouse_Blue "Porterhouse Blue")*.[[209]](#cite_note-214)

Other notable examples of the University of Cambridge in popular culture include:

* [Xu Zhimo](/wiki/Xu_Zhimo "Xu Zhimo")'s best-known poem, "On Leaving Cambridge" ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 再别康桥; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 再別康橋), published in 1928, is part of [China's national curriculum](/wiki/Education_in_China "Education in China") taught to all schoolchildren and has generated a tremendous amount of adoration of the University of Cambridge in China.[[210]](#cite_note-215)
* In the *[Psmith](/wiki/Psmith "Psmith")* series, a collection of novels published between 1908 and 1923 by [P. G. Wodehouse](/wiki/P._G._Wodehouse "P. G. Wodehouse"), the [title character](/wiki/Rupert_Psmith "Rupert Psmith") and [Mike](/wiki/Michael_%22Mike%22_Jackson "Michael \"Mike\" Jackson"), his closest friend, study at the University of Cambridge.
* The 1981 film *[Chariots of Fire](/wiki/Chariots_of_Fire "Chariots of Fire")* is partly set at Cambridge between 1919 and 1924 when protagonist [Harold Abrahams](/wiki/Harold_Abrahams "Harold Abrahams") (played by [Ben Cross](/wiki/Ben_Cross "Ben Cross")) was a student there.
* The 1983 film *[Monty Python's The Meaning of Life](/wiki/Monty_Python%27s_The_Meaning_of_Life "Monty Python's The Meaning of Life")* features [Churchill College](/wiki/Churchill_College,_Cambridge "Churchill College, Cambridge") in the film's church scene.
* The film *[Maurice](/wiki/Maurice_(1987_film) "Maurice (1987 film)")*, based on the 1971 novel [Maurice](/wiki/Maurice_(novel) "Maurice (novel)"), is partly filmed at the University of Cambridge.
* The 1989 film *[Dead Poets Society](/wiki/Dead_Poets_Society "Dead Poets Society")* features character John Keating (played by [Robin Williams](/wiki/Robin_Williams "Robin Williams")), who is a Welton Academy English teacher described in his senior annual as "Cambridge bound".
* [King's College](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge") of the University of Cambridge features prominently in the 1990 short story "[The Fenstanton Witch](/wiki/The_Fenstanton_Witch "The Fenstanton Witch")" by [M. R. James](/wiki/M._R._James "M. R. James").[[211]](#cite_note-Joshi-216)[[212]](#cite_note-Pardoe-217)
* The 1996 film *[True Blue](/wiki/True_Blue_(1996_film) "True Blue (1996 film)")* centres around a mutiny at the time of the 1985 [Oxford-Cambridge rowing race](/wiki/Boat_Race "Boat Race").
* The television series *[Frasier](/wiki/Frasier "Frasier")* (1993–2004) features [Niles Crane](/wiki/Niles_Crane "Niles Crane"), portrayed by [David Hyde Pierce](/wiki/David_Hyde_Pierce "David Hyde Pierce"), who studied as a graduate student at Cambridge.
* In the television series *[The Big Bang Theory](/wiki/The_Big_Bang_Theory "The Big Bang Theory")* (2007–2019), [Raj Koothrappali](/wiki/Raj_Koothrappali "Raj Koothrappali"), portrayed by [Kunal Nayyar](/wiki/Kunal_Nayyar "Kunal Nayyar"), studied astrophysics at Cambridge, and [Priya Koothrappali](/wiki/List_of_The_Big_Bang_Theory_and_Young_Sheldon_characters "List of The Big Bang Theory and Young Sheldon characters"), portrayed by [Aarti Mann](/wiki/Aarti_Mann "Aarti Mann"), studied law at Cambridge.
* In the 2002 film *[28 Days Later](/wiki/28_Days_Later "28 Days Later")*, a chimpanzee carrying the "Rage" virus is freed from a fictional Cambridge laboratory.
* The 2008 film *[The History Boys](/wiki/The_History_Boys_(film) "The History Boys (film)")* is about a group of boys applying to study history at Cambridge and [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford").
* In the 2011 film *[Sherlock Holmes: A Game of Shadows](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes:_A_Game_of_Shadows "Sherlock Holmes: A Game of Shadows")*, [Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes") is shown meeting his nemesis, [Professor Moriarty](/wiki/Professor_Moriarty "Professor Moriarty"), in Moriarty's office with a brief stock shot establishing it as [King's College](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge"), where Moriarty is a professor.
* In the 2014 biographical film *[The Theory of Everything](/wiki/The_Theory_of_Everything_(2014_film) "The Theory of Everything (2014 film)")*, young [Stephen Hawking](/wiki/Stephen_Hawking "Stephen Hawking") falls in love with literature student Jane Wilde at the University of Cambridge, where both of them study.
* The 2014 film *[The Imitation Game](/wiki/The_Imitation_Game "The Imitation Game")* is a historical drama that features [Alan Turing](/wiki/Alan_Turing "Alan Turing") (played by [Benedict Cumberbatch](/wiki/Benedict_Cumberbatch "Benedict Cumberbatch")), who is referenced as being a Cambridge alumnus and fellow at [King's College](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge") at Cambridge.
* The 2015 film *[The Man Who Knew Infinity](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Knew_Infinity "The Man Who Knew Infinity")* about mathematician [Srinivasa Ramanujan](/wiki/Srinivasa_Ramanujan "Srinivasa Ramanujan") was filmed at [Trinity College, Cambridge](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Cambridge "Trinity College, Cambridge"), where Ramanujan was a fellow.
* *[Red Joan](/wiki/Red_Joan "Red Joan")* (2018) was partly filmed at the University of Cambridge. Joan Smith (played by [Judi Dench](/wiki/Judi_Dench "Judi Dench")), loosely inspired by [Melita Norwood](/wiki/Melita_Norwood "Melita Norwood"), is depicted as studying physics at Cambridge.
* The 2021 film *[The Dig](/wiki/The_Dig_(2021_film) "The Dig (2021 film)")* features multiple [archaeologists](/wiki/Archaeologists "Archaeologists") with ties to the University of Cambridge, including [Charles Phillips](/wiki/Charles_Phillips_(archaeologist) "Charles Phillips (archaeologist)") (played by [Ken Stott](/wiki/Ken_Stott "Ken Stott")) and [Peggy Piggott](/wiki/Peggy_Guido "Peggy Guido") (played by [Lily James](/wiki/Lily_James "Lily James")).
* The [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_Network) "ITV (TV Network)") television series *[Granchester](/wiki/Grantchester_(TV_series) "Grantchester (TV series)")* (2014–) is partly set in and filmed at Cambridge.
* The television series *[Professor T](/wiki/Professor_T._(British_TV_series) "Professor T. (British TV series)")* (2021–) stars [Ben Miller](/wiki/Ben_Miller "Ben Miller") as Professor Jasper Tempest, a genius University of Cambridge criminologist.
* The 2023 film *[Oppenheimer](/wiki/Oppenheimer_(film) "Oppenheimer (film)")* contains establishing shots of [King's College](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge") filmed by the [River Cam](/wiki/River_Cam "River Cam").[[213]](#cite_note-218) However, [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer") actually studied at [Christ's College](/wiki/Christ%27s_College,_Cambridge "Christ's College, Cambridge").

1. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** Refers to operational estate owned by the university, including the 550-hectare (1,400-acre) University Farm[[9]](#cite_note-9) but not including college-owned property or investment properties
2. **[^](#cite_ref-110)** Not be confused solely with [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British")
3. **[^](#cite_ref-111)** Includes those who indicate that they identify as [Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian"), [Black](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people"), [Mixed Heritage](/wiki/Mixed_(United_Kingdom_ethnicity_category) "Mixed (United Kingdom ethnicity category)"), [Arab](/wiki/British_Arabs "British Arabs") or any other ethnicity except White.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-113)** Calculated from the Polar4 measure, using Quintile1, in England and Wales. Calculated from the [Scottish Index of Multiple Deprivation](/wiki/Scottish_Index_of_Multiple_Deprivation "Scottish Index of Multiple Deprivation") (SIMD) measure, using SIMD20, in Scotland.
